> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams?** The physiological geometry of the heart is complex and cannot be faithfully rendered in a flow-based diagram. The above **block-level functional architecture** maps the *cause-and-effect* relationships of heart sound and murmur generation — which is the engineering-relevant perspective for biomedical instrumentation design.

### 4.4 Phonocardiogram (PCG) Trace — Annotated Schematic

```
  Amplitude
     │
  S1 │  ████                ████
  S2 │       ██            ██
  S3 │        ░░               ░░
  S4 │ ▒                       ▒
     └──────────────────────────────────► Time
        |S1|      |S2|         |S1|
        |←─ Systole (~0.3s) ─→|
        |←──── Diastole (~0.5s) ──────→|
```

- **S1** = Tall, sharp spike (closure of AV valves)
- **S2** = Tall, sharp spike (closure of semilunar valves)
- **S3** = Soft, low-amplitude bump in early diastole
- **S4** = Soft, low-amplitude bump in late diastole (pre-S1)
- **Murmurs** appear as **prolonged vibrations** between S1 and S2 (systolic) or between S2 and S1 (diastolic).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question 2024 Scheme]**
**Define heart sounds. Explain the mechanism of production of the first and second heart sounds with their characteristic features.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Heart sounds are the **audible vibrations** produced by the closure of cardiac valves, vibration of cardio-hemic structures, and acceleration/deceleration of blood during the cardiac cycle.

**Mechanism of S1 (1 Mark):**
- S1 marks the **onset of ventricular systole**.
- It is produced by the **closure of the atrioventricular (AV) valves** — the **mitral (M2 component)** and **tricuspid (T1 component)** valves.
- The leaflets abruptly decelerate, transferring kinetic energy to the closed valve apparatus and surrounding blood, producing a low-frequency sound (~40 Hz).
- The mnemonic is "**Lub**" — a long, soft, low-pitched sound.

**Mechanism of S2 (1 Mark):**
- S2 marks the **end of ventricular systole / onset of diastole**.
- It is produced by the **closure of the semilunar valves** — the **aortic (A2)** and **pulmonary (P2)** valves.
- It is a sharper, higher-pitched, shorter sound (~50 Hz).
- The mnemonic is "**Dub**" — short, sharp, higher-pitched.

> [!NOTE]
> A2 normally precedes P2 slightly because LV ejection ends before RV ejection. Wide splitting of S2 occurs in RBBB; paradoxical splitting in aortic stenosis.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question 2024 Scheme]**
**What are heart murmurs? Classify them based on timing in the cardiac cycle with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Heart murmurs are **prolonged, audible vibrations** caused by **turbulent blood flow** through stenotic, regurgitant, or shunt lesions.

**Classification (2 Marks):**

1. **Systolic Murmurs** (between S1 and S2):
   - *Mid-systolic (ejection) murmur:* Aortic stenosis
   - *Holosystolic murmur:* Mitral regurgitation

2. **Diastolic Murmurs** (between S2 and S1):
   - *Early diastolic murmur:* Aortic regurgitation
   - *Mid-diastolic murmur:* Mitral stenosis

3. **Continuous Murmurs** (throughout systole and diastole):
   - Patent Ductus Arteriosus (PDA)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme: Part B Module 3 questions are typically of 14 marks with internal choice. Each sub-part carries 7 marks. Full diagrams and derivations are mandatory for full marks.

---

## Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Explain the physiological mechanism of production of **S1, S2, S3, and S4** heart sounds, including their **timing, location, and clinical significance.** *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **Levine grading system** for murmur intensity. With a neat diagram of the **cardiac cycle**, mark and label the timing of all four heart sounds and identify the locations of **systolic, diastolic, and continuous murmurs.** *(7 marks)*

### Model Answer for Q-A(a):

**1. S1 — First Heart Sound (Lub):** *(2 marks)*
- **Mechanism:** Closure of **mitral** and **tricuspid AV valves** at the onset of ventricular systole (isovolumetric contraction phase).
- **Timing:** Marks the beginning of systole; ~0.05 s after QRS complex on ECG.
- **Location:** Best heard at the **apex (mitral area, 5th ICS, midclavicular line)**.
- **Components:** M1 (mitral) followed by T1 (tricuspid) — usually merged.
- **Clinical Significance:**
  - **Loud S1** → Mitral stenosis (leaflets wide open, slam shut).
  - **Soft S1** → Mitral regurgitation, severe aortic regurgitation, calcified mitral valve.
  - **Variable S1** → Atrial fibrillation, complete heart block.

**2. S2 — Second Heart Sound (Dub):** *(2 marks)*
- **Mechanism:** Closure of **aortic (A2)** and **pulmonary (P2)** semilunar valves at the end of ventricular systole.
- **Timing:** Marks the end of systole / beginning of diastole.
- **Location:** Best heard at the **2nd right ICS (aortic area)** and **2nd left ICS (pulmonary area)**.
- **Components:** A2 (louder, earlier) and P2 (softer, later). Physiological splitting on inspiration.
- **Clinical Significance:**
  - **Wide fixed splitting** → ASD.
  - **Paradoxical splitting** → Aortic stenosis, LBBB.
  - **Loud P2** → Pulmonary hypertension.

**3. S3 — Third Heart Sound (Ventricular Gallop):** *(1.5 marks)*
- **Mechanism:** Rapid ventricular filling in early diastole — the deceleration of blood against the ventricular wall causes vibration.
- **Timing:** ~0.12–0.18 s after S2.
- **Location:** Best heard at **apex**, in **left lateral decubitus position**, with the **bell of the stethoscope**.
- **Clinical Significance:**
  - **Physiological** in children, young adults, pregnancy.
  - **Pathological** in adults >40: **volume overload** — heart failure (systolic dysfunction), MR, AR, dilated cardiomyopathy.

**4. S4 — Fourth Heart Sound (Atrial Gallop):** *(1.5 marks)*
- **Mechanism:** Atrial contraction forces blood into a **stiff, non-compliant ventricle** — vibrations occur as blood strikes the rigid wall.
- **Timing:** Just before S1 (late diastole).
- **Location:** Best heard at the **apex**, in left lateral decubitus, with bell.
- **Clinical Significance:** **Always pathological** — pressure overload: hypertension, aortic stenosis, HCM, ischemic heart disease.

### Model Answer for Q-A(b):

**Levine Grading System for Murmur Intensity (3 marks):**

| Grade | Description | Thrill? |
|---|---|---|
| **1/6** | Heard only with intense concentration in a quiet room | No |
| **2/6** | Soft, but immediately audible | No |
| **3/6** | Moderately loud | No |
| **4/6** | Loud, accompanied by palpable thrill | Yes |
| **5/6** | Very loud, heard with stethoscope edge off chest | Yes |
| **6/6** | Heard without stethoscope | Yes |

**Cardiac Cycle Diagram with Murmur Timing (4 marks):**

*(Refer to Section 4.4 Phonocardiogram schematic)*

| Region | Acoustic Event | Clinical Examples |
|---|---|---|
| S1 to mid-systole | Mid-systolic (ejection) murmurs | Aortic stenosis, HCM |
| S1 to S2 (full) | Holosystolic murmurs | Mitral regurgitation, VSD, TR |
| S2 to mid-diastole | Early & mid-diastolic murmurs | Aortic regurgitation, MS |
| S2 to S1 (continuous) | Continuous murmurs | PDA, AV fistula |

---

## Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** With the help of the **Reynolds number equation**, derive the threshold for turbulent flow in a stenotic aortic valve. Use the data: normal aortic valve area = 3 cm², stenotic area = 0.5 cm², cardiac output = 5 L/min. Assume blood density = 1060 kg/m³ and viscosity = 3.5 × 10⁻³ Pa·s. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **engineering principles of a Phonocardiograph (PCG)** including its block diagram, sensor types, frequency ranges, and signal processing techniques used for murmur detection. *(7 marks)*

### Model Answer for Q-B(a):

**Step 1 — Convert units and define the Reynolds number:** *(1 mark)*

$$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}$$

with $\rho = 1060\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$.

**Step 2 — Compute cardiac output (Q) in m³/s:** *(1 mark)*

$$Q = 5\,\text{L/min} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 8.33 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute normal velocity:** *(1 mark)*

$$A_n = 3\,\text{cm}^2 = 3 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2$$

$$v_n = \frac{Q}{A_n} = \frac{8.33 \times 10^{-5}}{3 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 0.278\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute stenotic velocity using continuity:** *(1 mark)*

$$A_s = 0.5\,\text{cm}^2 = 5 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2$$

$$v_s = \frac{Q}{A_s} = \frac{8.33 \times 10^{-5}}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.667\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Estimate stenotic diameter:** *(0.5 marks)*

$$D_s = 2\sqrt{\frac{A_s}{\pi}} = 2\sqrt{\frac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{3.1416}} = 2\sqrt{1.592 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 0.00798\,\text{m} \approx 8\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Reynolds number at stenosis:** *(1 mark)*

$$Re = \frac{(1060)(1.667)(0.00798)}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{14.10}{0.0035} \approx 4028$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:** *(1.5 marks)*

Since $Re = 4028 > Re_{crit} \approx 2000$, the flow at the stenosis is **turbulent**. This turbulence produces a **systolic ejection murmur**, with intensity increasing with $(Re - Re_{crit})$. Doppler echo would estimate a pressure gradient of $\Delta P = 4v^2 = 4(1.667)^2 \approx 11.1\,\text{mmHg}$ (mild; severe >40 mmHg).

### Model Answer for Q-B(b):

**Phonocardiograph (PCG) — Engineering Principles (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A phonocardiograph is an instrument that graphically records heart sounds and murmurs via sensors placed on the chest wall, enabling time-amplitude analysis of cardiac acoustic events.

**Sensor Types (1.5 marks):**
- **Electronic Stethoscope (microphone-based):** Air-coupled condenser or piezoelectric microphone; captures airborne vibrations.
- **Accelerometer (contact):** Senses chest wall vibration directly; better low-frequency response.
- **Seismocardiograph (SCG):** 3-axis MEMS accelerometer; records chest wall motion.
- **Implantable:** Leadless intracardiac microphones (research stage).

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

*(Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid Diagram)*

Sensor → Preamplifier (×1000) → Band-Pass Filter (20–1000 Hz) → ADC (≥2000 Hz) → DSP → Display

**Frequency Range Mapping (1.5 marks):**

| Acoustic Event | Frequency Range |
|---|---|
| S1, S2 | 20–150 Hz |
| S3, S4 | 15–70 Hz |
| Mid-systolic (AS) | 150–800 Hz |
| Mid-diastolic rumble (MS) | 50–300 Hz |
| Early diastolic (AR) | High-pitched blowing |

**Signal Processing Techniques (1 mark):**
- **Time-domain:** Envelope detection, autocorrelation.
- **Frequency-domain:** **FFT**, **Power Spectral Density (PSD)**.
- **Time-frequency:** **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)**, **Wavelet Transform (CWT/DWT)**.
- **AI-based:** CNN/LSTM on log-Mel spectrograms for automated murmur classification (e.g., PhysioNet 2022 Challenge).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the timing context:** Always tie S1/S2/S3/S4 to the **cardiac cycle phase** — students lose 1–2 marks for not mentioning "systole/diastole" placement.
> 2. **Confusing S3 and S4:** S3 = early diastole (rapid filling), S4 = late diastole (atrial kick). Many students invert this.
> 3. **Forgetting units in Reynolds derivation:** Always carry units of $\rho$ in kg/m³, $v$ in m/s, $D$ in m, and $\mu$ in Pa·s to make $Re$ dimensionless. Examiners deduct 1 mark if final $Re$ is left without verification of dimensionless nature.
> 4. **Murmur location omissions:** The Levine grade and best auscultation area must both be mentioned for full marks.
> 5. **Bernoulli constant:** The "4" in $\Delta P = 4v^2$ comes from $\rho/(2 \times 133.32) \approx 3.98$ — show this derivation in full.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Heart Sounds & Murmurs**

- 🔑 **S1 = "Lub"** = AV valve closure (Mitral + Tricuspid) → Onset of systole
- 🔑 **S2 = "Dub"** = Semilunar valve closure (Aortic + Pulmonary) → End of systole
- 🔑 **S3 = Early diastole** = Rapid ventricular filling → Physiological in young, pathological (volume overload) in adults
- 🔑 **S4 = Late diastole** = Atrial kick into stiff ventricle → **Always pathological** (pressure overload)
- 🔑 **Mnemonic:** S4–S1–S2 = "**TEN-nes-see**" | S1–S2–S3 = "**KEN-tucky**"
- 🔑 **Turbulence criterion:** $Re > 2000$ → murmur generated
- 🔑 **Bernoulli (clinical):** $\Delta P \approx 4v^2$ (mmHg, $v$ in m/s)
- 🔑 **Cardiac cycle at HR=75:** $T = 0.8\,\text{s}$ (Systole 0.3s + Diastole 0.5s)
- 🔑 **Levine grades:** 1 (barely) → 6 (audible without stethoscope); thrill appears at grade 4
- 🔑 **Holosystolic murmurs:** MR, TR, VSD — uniform intensity throughout systole
- 🔑 **Ejection (crescendo–decrescendo) murmurs:** AS, PS, HCM
- 🔑 **Early diastolic decrescendo:** Aortic regurgitation, Pulmonary regurgitation
- 🔑 **Mid-diastolic rumble with presystolic accentuation:** Mitral stenosis
- 🔑 **Continuous "machinery" murmur:** PDA
- 🔑 **PCG signal chain:** Sensor → Preamp (×1000) → BPF (20–1000 Hz) → ADC (≥2000 Hz) → DSP → Display
- 🔑 **PCG sampling rate** must be ≥2000 Hz (Nyquist for highest murmur frequency)
- 🔑 **Frequencies:** S1/S2: 20–150 Hz | S3/S4: 15–70 Hz | Murmurs: up to 1000 Hz
- 🔑 **A2 before P2** normally; wide splitting → ASD/RBBB; paradoxical splitting → AS/LBBB
- 🔑 **Re formula:** $Re = \rho v D / \mu$ — dimensionless verification mandatory in KTU derivations
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stroke Volume & Cardiac Output
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT205 - ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING (BIOMEDICAL AND ROBOTIC ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stroke Volume & Cardiac Output

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Stroke Volume (SV) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stroke Volume (SV)** is defined as the volume of blood ejected by **each ventricle** during **one complete cardiac cycle** (i.e., per single heartbeat). It represents the *net working output* of the heart's pumping chamber and is measured in **milliliters per beat (mL/beat)**.

In the resting adult human, the standard accepted reference value is approximately **70 mL/beat** (range typically **60–100 mL/beat**). Stroke volume is fundamentally the *difference* between the volume of blood present in the ventricle at the end of diastole (filling) and the volume remaining at the end of systole (contraction).

$$SV = EDV - ESV$$

Where:
- **EDV** = End-Diastolic Volume (volume in ventricle just before contraction) — typically **~120 mL**
- **ESV** = End-Systolic Volume (volume remaining after contraction) — typically **~50 mL**

### 1.2 Cardiac Output (CO) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cardiac Output (CO)** is the *total volume of blood pumped by each ventricle per unit time*, conventionally expressed in **liters per minute (L/min)**. For a resting adult, the canonical reference value is approximately **5 L/min**.

$$CO = HR \times SV$$

Where:
- **HR** = Heart Rate (beats per minute, bpm) — typically **~70 bpm** at rest
- **SV** = Stroke Volume (mL/beat) — typically **~70 mL/beat**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the heart as a **mechanical water pump** with two identical chambers (the left and right ventricles). Now imagine a fuel-station attendant using a calibrated piston pump:

- The **Stroke Volume** is the amount of fuel drawn and expelled in *one full squeeze* of the handle. A bigger squeeze equals more fuel per cycle.
- The **Cardiac Output** is the *total fuel delivered per minute*. This depends on how *big* each squeeze is (SV) and how *fast* the attendant pumps the handle (HR).

If the pump (heart) needs to deliver more fuel (blood) during exercise, it can either **squeeze harder** (increase SV) or **pump faster** (increase HR) — or both. This dual-control mechanism is precisely how the human cardiovascular system meets the body's changing metabolic demands.

### 1.4 Key Clinical & Physiological Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Cardiac Index (CI)** — A normalized measure of CO accounting for body surface area (BSA):
> $$CI = \frac{CO}{BSA}$$
> - Standard resting value: **2.5 – 4.0 L/min/m²**
> - Used clinically to compare cardiac performance across patients of different body sizes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Ejection Fraction (EF)** — The *fraction* of end-diastolic volume ejected per beat, expressed as a percentage:
> $$EF = \frac{SV}{EDV} \times 100\%$$
> - Normal range: **55% – 70%**
> - Below 40% typically indicates **systolic heart failure**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Determinants of Stroke Volume

Stroke volume is governed by **three primary factors**, often referred to as the **"Big Three"** in cardiovascular physiology:

#### (a) Preload — The *Filling* Factor

- **Definition**: The degree of stretch of the ventricular myocardium at the end of diastole.
- **Clinical surrogate**: End-Diastolic Volume (EDV) or End-Diastolic Pressure (EDP).
- **Determinants of preload itself**:
  - Venous return (volume of blood returning to the heart)
  - Total blood volume
  - Venous tone (compliance of systemic veins)
  - Atrial contraction ("atrial kick" contributes ~20–30% of ventricular filling)
- **Mechanism**: Greater preload → greater stretch of sarcomeres → more optimal actin-myosin cross-bridge formation → stronger contraction.

#### (b) Afterload — The *Resistance* Factor

- **Definition**: The pressure the ventricle must overcome to eject blood during systole.
- **Clinical surrogate**: Aortic pressure (for LV) or pulmonary artery pressure (for RV).
- **Determinants of afterload**:
  - Systemic vascular resistance (SVR)
  - Arterial blood pressure
  - Aortic valve resistance
  - Blood viscosity
- **Mechanism**: Higher afterload → ventricle must generate more force → reduced stroke volume (if contractility is fixed).

#### (c) Contractility — The *Muscle Strength* Factor

- **Definition**: The intrinsic strength of the ventricular contraction at any given preload and afterload.
- **Independent of preload and afterload** — this is the *inotropic state*.
- **Determinants of contractility**:
  - Sympathetic nervous system stimulation (norepinephrine → β₁ receptors)
  - Circulating catecholamines (epinephrine)
  - Calcium availability (extracellular Ca²⁺ influx + SR Ca²⁺ release)
  - Drugs: Positive inotropes (digoxin, dobutamine) vs. Negative inotropes (β-blockers)

### 2.2 The Frank-Starling Mechanism (Law of the Heart)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frank-Starling Law of the Heart**: *"The energy of contraction is proportional to the initial length of the cardiac muscle fiber."* In practical terms: **the greater the ventricular filling (preload), the greater the stroke volume — up to a physiological limit.**

This is the **fundamental intrinsic regulatory mechanism** of the heart. It allows the heart to automatically match its output to venous return without requiring neural input.

### 2.3 Factors Affecting Cardiac Output

Cardiac output is a *systemic* variable influenced by:

| Factor | Effect on CO | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| ↑ Heart Rate | ↑ CO | More cycles per minute (up to ~120–140 bpm) |
| ↑ Stroke Volume | ↑ CO | Greater ejection per beat |
| ↑ Venous Return | ↑ CO (via ↑ SV) | Increases preload → Frank-Starling |
| ↑ Sympathetic activity | ↑ CO | ↑ HR + ↑ Contractility |
| ↑ Parasympathetic activity | ↓ CO | ↓ HR (dominant effect on SA node) |
| Exercise | ↑ CO (up to 5–6×) | Combined ↑ HR + ↑ SV + ↓ SVR |
| Blood loss / hypovolemia | ↓ CO | ↓ Preload → ↓ SV |
| Heart failure | ↓ CO | ↓ Contractility → ↓ SV |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units / Normal Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $SV = EDV - ESV$ | Stroke Volume (basic definition) | **70 mL/beat** |
| 2 | $CO = HR \times SV$ | Cardiac Output (Fick principle, basic) | **5 L/min** |
| 3 | $EF = \frac{SV}{EDV} \times 100\%$ | Ejection Fraction | **55–70 %** |
| 4 | $CI = \frac{CO}{BSA}$ | Cardiac Index | **2.5–4.0 L/min/m²** |
| 5 | $CO_{exercise} \approx 5 \times CO_{rest}$ | Maximal cardiac output during exercise | **20–25 L/min** (athletes: up to **35 L/min**) |
| 6 | $MAP = CO \times SVR + CVP$ | Mean Arterial Pressure relationship | **70–100 mmHg** |
| 7 | $P = \frac{Q \times 8 \eta L}{\pi r^4}$ | Poiseuille's Law (governs SVR/afterload) | $P$ = pressure, $Q$ = flow, $\eta$ = viscosity, $L$ = length, $r$ = radius |

> [!NOTE]
> In all KTU board examinations, the most frequently tested formulas are **(1)**, **(2)**, and **(3)**. Memorize these as your *core equations* — they appear in nearly every past paper.

### 2.5 Real-World Biomedical Engineering Utility

Understanding stroke volume and cardiac output is foundational for designing:

- **Implantable Pacemakers** — Closed-loop systems that adapt pacing rate based on measured SV/CO sensors.
- **Left Ventricular Assist Devices (LVADs)** — Mechanical circulatory support that supplements native CO in heart failure patients.
- **Bioimpedance / Impedance Cardiography Devices** — Non-invasive SV measurement using thoracic electrical impedance changes synchronized with the cardiac cycle.
- **Wearable Fitness Trackers** — Modern photoplethysmography (PPG) algorithms estimate SV from pulse waveform morphology.
- **Heart Failure Diagnostic Software** — EF thresholds (40%, 35%, 30%) drive clinical decisions for ICD/CRT-D implantation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cardiac Output from First Principles

We begin from the **physiological premise** that the heart ejects a discrete volume of blood (SV) with every beat, and the heart beats at a discrete rate (HR) every minute.

**Step 1 — Define the per-beat ejection volume.**

The ventricle, at the end of diastole, contains blood equal to the **end-diastolic volume (EDV)**. After contraction, a residual volume equal to the **end-systolic volume (ESV)** remains. Therefore, the volume ejected per beat is:

$$SV = EDV - ESV$$

*Explanation:* This is a simple mass-balance argument — the volume *entering* the chamber minus the volume *remaining* equals the volume *ejected*.

**Step 2 — Define the per-minute cycle count.**

If the heart beats at $HR$ beats per minute, then in one minute, the ventricle completes exactly $HR$ contraction cycles.

**Step 3 — Multiply per-beat output by per-minute cycles.**

The total volume ejected per minute by one ventricle is therefore:

$$CO = SV \times HR$$

*Explanation:* This is a direct application of the *flow rate = volume × frequency* principle used universally in fluid mechanics.

**Step 4 — Express in standard SI-compatible units.**

Converting mL to liters (1 L = 1000 mL), the resting cardiac output evaluates as:

$$CO = 70 \, \frac{mL}{beat} \times 70 \, \frac{beats}{min} = 4900 \, \frac{mL}{min} \approx 4.9 \, \frac{L}{min} \approx 5 \, \frac{L}{min}$$

**Step 5 — Normalize for body size to obtain Cardiac Index.**

For a typical adult with Body Surface Area (BSA) $\approx 1.7 \, m^2$:

$$CI = \frac{CO}{BSA} = \frac{5 \, L/min}{1.7 \, m^2} \approx 2.94 \, \frac{L}{min \cdot m^2}$$

This falls within the normal physiological range of **2.5–4.0 L/min/m²**. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation: Ejection Fraction

Ejection fraction is defined as the *fractional* utilization of the end-diastolic volume:

**Step 1 — Define the ratio.**

$$EF = \frac{\text{Volume Ejected}}{\text{Volume Available at End of Diastole}} = \frac{SV}{EDV}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by 100% for percentage form.**

$$EF = \frac{SV}{EDV} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the resting numerical values.**

$$EF = \frac{70 \, mL}{120 \, mL} \times 100\% = 58.3\%$$

This is well within the normal range of **55–70%**. ✓

**Step 4 — Express EF in terms of EDV and ESV.**

Since $SV = EDV - ESV$:

$$EF = \frac{EDV - ESV}{EDV} \times 100\% = \left(1 - \frac{ESV}{EDV}\right) \times 100\%$$

This alternative form is often tested in KTU board exams as a *trick question*.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Numerical Problem-Solving Patterns

| Problem Type | Given Variables | Required Output | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type A**: Basic CO calculation | HR, SV | CO | $CO = HR \times SV$ |
| **Type B**: Reverse CO calculation | CO, HR | SV | $SV = \frac{CO}{HR}$ |
| **Type C**: EF computation | SV, EDV | EF | $EF = \frac{SV}{EDV} \times 100\%$ |
| **Type D**: EDV-ESV relation | EDV, ESV | SV | $SV = EDV - ESV$ |
| **Type E**: Cardiac Index | CO, BSA, height, weight | CI | $CI = \frac{CO}{BSA}$, with Mosteller formula: $BSA = \sqrt{\frac{H \times W}{3600}}$ |
| **Type F**: Exercise CO | Resting CO, exercise multiplier | Max CO | $CO_{max} = k \times CO_{rest}$ where $k \approx 5$ (untrained), $\approx 7$ (athletes) |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> **Problem**: A patient has a heart rate of 75 bpm, end-diastolic volume of 130 mL, and end-systolic volume of 55 mL. Calculate: (a) Stroke Volume, (b) Cardiac Output, (c) Ejection Fraction, (d) Cardiac Index assuming BSA = 1.8 m².

**Solution — Part (a): Stroke Volume**

$$SV = EDV - ESV = 130 - 55 = 75 \, \text{mL/beat}$$

**Solution — Part (b): Cardiac Output**

$$CO = HR \times SV = 75 \times 75 = 5625 \, \text{mL/min} \approx 5.625 \, \text{L/min}$$

**Solution — Part (c): Ejection Fraction**

$$EF = \frac{SV}{EDV} \times 100\% = \frac{75}{130} \times 100\% \approx 57.7\%$$

**Solution — Part (d): Cardiac Index**

$$CI = \frac{CO}{BSA} = \frac{5.625}{1.8} \approx 3.125 \, \frac{L}{min \cdot m^2}$$

All values are **within normal physiological limits**. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Determinants of Stroke Volume — Functional Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STROKE VOLUME SV] --> B[PRELOAD]
    A --> C[AFTERLOAD]
    A --> D[CONTRACTILITY]
    B --> B1[Venous Return]
    B --> B2[Blood Volume]
    B --> B3[Atrial Kick]
    B --> B4[Venous Tone]
    C --> C1[Aortic Pressure]
    C --> C2[Systemic Vascular Resistance]
    C --> C3[Arterial Compliance]
    D --> D1[Sympathetic NS Activity]
    D --> D2[Calcium Availability]
    D --> D3[Inotropic Drugs]
    B --> E[FRANK STARLING MECHANISM]
    C --> F[VENTRICULAR WALL TENSION]
    D --> G[MYOCARDIAL FORCE]
    E --> H[STROKE VOLUME OUTPUT 70 ML]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[CARDIAC OUTPUT CO = HR x SV]
```

### 4.2 Cardiac Output Regulation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        X1[Venous Return]
        X2[Sympathetic Tone]
        X3[Parasympathetic Tone]
        X4[Blood Volume]
        X5[Arterial Pressure]
    end

        Y1[SA Node HR]
        Y2[Ventricular Preload]
        Y3[Ventricular Afterload]
        Y4[Myocardial Contractility]
    end

        Z1[Stroke Volume SV]
        Z2[Cardiac Output CO]
        Z3[Ejection Fraction EF]
        Z4[Cardiac Index CI]
    end

    X2 --> Y1
    X2 --> Y4
    X3 --> Y1
    X4 --> Y2
    X5 --> Y3
    Y1 --> Z2
    Y2 --> Z1
    Y3 --> Z1
    Y4 --> Z1
    Z1 --> Z2
    Z1 --> Z3
    Z2 --> Z4
```

### 4.3 Volume Cycle of the Left Ventricle (Wiggers-Style Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Diastole Begins] --> P2[Ventricular Filling Phase]
    P2 --> P3[EDV approximately 120 ML at End of Diastole]
    P3 --> P4[Isovolumetric Contraction]
    P4 --> P5[Aortic Valve Opens]
    P5 --> P6[Ventricular Ejection Phase]
    P6 --> P7[ESV approximately 50 ML at End of Systole]
    P7 --> P8[Isovolumetric Relaxation]
    P8 --> P9[Diastole Begins Cycle Repeats]
    P3 --> Q1[Stroke Volume SV equals EDV minus ESV equals 70 ML]
    P7 --> Q1
    Q1 --> R1[Cardiac Output CO equals HR times SV equals 5 L per min]
```

### 4.4 Frank-Starling Curve — Functional Block Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Preload] --> B[Low Sarcomere Stretch]
    B --> C[Suboptimal Cross Bridge Formation]
    C --> D[Low Force of Contraction]
    D --> E[Low Stroke Volume]
    F[Optimal Preload] --> G[Optimal Sarcomere Stretch approximately 2.2 micrometer]
    G --> H[Maximum Cross Bridge Formation]
    H --> I[Maximum Force of Contraction]
    I --> J[Maximum Stroke Volume]
    K[Excessive Preload] --> L[Overstretch of Sarcomeres]
    L --> M[Reduced Cross Bridge Formation]
    M --> N[Reduced Force of Contraction]
    N --> O[Reduced Stroke Volume Heart Failure Risk]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Cardiac Output. State its normal value and the formula used to calculate it.**

**Model Answer:**
Cardiac Output (CO) is defined as the volume of blood pumped out by each ventricle per minute. It is calculated using the formula:

$$CO = HR \times SV$$

where HR is heart rate (beats/min) and SV is stroke volume (mL/beat). The normal resting value for an adult is approximately **5 L/min** (≈ 4.9–5.0 L/min).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Ejection Fraction. Mention its normal range and clinical significance.**

**Model Answer:**
Ejection Fraction (EF) is the percentage of end-diastolic volume that is ejected during each ventricular contraction. It is given by:

$$EF = \frac{SV}{EDV} \times 100\%$$

The normal EF range is **55% – 70%**. Clinically, EF below 40% indicates systolic dysfunction / heart failure; below 30% indicates severe failure often requiring device therapy (ICD/LVAD).

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Choose ONE)

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **Frank-Starling Law of the Heart**. Describe the three primary determinants of Stroke Volume with suitable physiological diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A patient presents with the following cardiac parameters:
- Heart Rate = 80 bpm
- End-Diastolic Volume = 125 mL
- End-Systolic Volume = 45 mL
- Body Surface Area = 1.75 m²

Calculate **(i)** Stroke Volume, **(ii)** Cardiac Output, **(iii)** Ejection Fraction, and **(iv)** Cardiac Index. Comment on whether each value lies within normal physiological limits. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The **Frank-Starling Law** states that the energy of contraction of cardiac muscle is proportional to the initial length (stretch) of the muscle fiber. In practical terms:

> *"The greater the volume of blood in the ventricle during diastole (preload), the greater the force of contraction during systole, and therefore the greater the stroke volume — up to an optimal physiological limit."*

**Mechanism**: Increased venous return → increased EDV → increased sarcomere stretch (toward optimal ~2.2 μm) → optimal actin-myosin overlap → increased number of cross-bridges → increased contractile force → increased SV.

**Three Determinants of Stroke Volume**:

1. **Preload** — Degree of ventricular stretch at end-diastole. Governed by venous return, blood volume, and venous compliance. ↑ Preload → ↑ SV (Frank-Starling).

2. **Afterload** — Pressure the ventricle must overcome to eject blood. Determined by systemic vascular resistance (SVR) and arterial blood pressure. ↑ Afterload → ↓ SV (for fixed contractility).

3. **Contractility** — Intrinsic strength of myocardial contraction. Independent of preload/afterload. Enhanced by sympathetic stimulation (β₁ receptors) and calcium availability. ↑ Contractility → ↑ SV.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Stroke Volume:**

$$SV = EDV - ESV = 125 - 45 = 80 \, mL/beat$$

**(ii) Cardiac Output:**

$$CO = HR \times SV = 80 \times 80 = 6400 \, mL/min = 6.4 \, L/min$$

**(iii) Ejection Fraction:**

$$EF = \frac{SV}{EDV} \times 100\% = \frac{80}{125} \times 100\% = 64\%$$

**(iv) Cardiac Index:**

$$CI = \frac{CO}{BSA} = \frac{6.4}{1.75} \approx 3.66 \, \frac{L}{min \cdot m^2}$$

**Clinical Interpretation:**

| Parameter | Calculated Value | Normal Range | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| SV | 80 mL/beat | 60–100 mL/beat | ✓ Normal |
| CO | 6.4 L/min | 4–6 L/min (rest) | ⚠ Mildly elevated |
| EF | 64% | 55–70% | ✓ Normal |
| CI | 3.66 L/min/m² | 2.5–4.0 L/min/m² | ✓ Normal |

The mildly elevated CO may indicate a hyperdynamic state (e.g., mild exercise, anxiety, or hyperthyroidism).

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Alternative Module Question]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Stroke Volume** and **Cardiac Output**. Explain any **four factors affecting Cardiac Output** in the human body. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between **Ejection Fraction, EDV, and ESV**. A healthy athlete has a resting heart rate of 50 bpm, EDV of 140 mL, and ESV of 40 mL. Compute his cardiac output, stroke volume, ejection fraction, and cardiac index (assume BSA = 1.9 m²). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Difference between Stroke Volume and Cardiac Output:**

| Feature | Stroke Volume (SV) | Cardiac Output (CO) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Volume ejected per beat | Volume ejected per minute |
| Unit | mL/beat | L/min |
| Normal Value | ~70 mL/beat | ~5 L/min |
| Dependence | Depends on EDV, ESV, contractility | Depends on HR and SV |
| Formula | $SV = EDV - ESV$ | $CO = HR \times SV$ |

**Four Factors Affecting Cardiac Output:**

1. **Heart Rate (HR)** — CO is directly proportional to HR. ↑ HR → ↑ CO, until very high rates reduce filling time.

2. **Stroke Volume (SV)** — CO is directly proportional to SV. ↑ SV → ↑ CO.

3. **Venous Return** — Greater venous return increases preload, which via the Frank-Starling mechanism increases SV and hence CO.

4. **Sympathetic Nervous System Activity** — Sympathetic stimulation increases both HR (positive chronotropy) and contractility (positive inotropy), thereby increasing CO. Parasympathetic (vagal) stimulation decreases HR, decreasing CO.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Derivation:**

Ejection Fraction is defined as the ratio of stroke volume to end-diastolic volume:

$$EF = \frac{SV}{EDV} \times 100\%$$

Since $SV = EDV - ESV$:

$$EF = \frac{EDV - ESV}{EDV} \times 100\%$$

Or equivalently:

$$EF = \left(1 - \frac{ESV}{EDV}\right) \times 100\%$$

**Numerical Calculations for the Athlete:**

**Stroke Volume:**

$$SV = EDV - ESV = 140 - 40 = 100 \, mL/beat$$

**Cardiac Output:**

$$CO = HR \times SV = 50 \times 100 = 5000 \, mL/min = 5.0 \, L/min$$

**Ejection Fraction:**

$$EF = \frac{100}{140} \times 100\% \approx 71.4\%$$

**Cardiac Index:**

$$CI = \frac{CO}{BSA} = \frac{5.0}{1.9} \approx 2.63 \, \frac{L}{min \cdot m^2}$$

**Interpretation**: The athlete demonstrates classic features of an *athlete's heart* — elevated SV, lower resting HR (bradycardia due to high vagal tone), and elevated EF. All parameters are within or above the normal range.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors**: Students frequently forget to convert mL/min to L/min in CO calculations. Always state the unit explicitly — examiners **deduct 1 full mark** for unit inconsistency.
> 2. **Confusing SV with EDV/ESV**: In part (a) of numerical questions, never confuse EDV with SV. SV = EDV − **ESV** (subtract, not add).
> 3. **Skipping the Formula Statement**: In 14-mark answers, **always write the formula before plugging in numbers**. The formula line carries 1–2 marks independently.
> 4. **Cardiac Index vs Cardiac Output**: Many students report CO without normalizing for BSA. If the question mentions BSA, **CI is mandatory**.
> 5. **EF as Percentage vs Fraction**: Always multiply by 100% and add the "%" symbol. Reporting EF as 0.58 instead of 58% loses 0.5–1 mark.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Stroke Volume (SV)** = Volume ejected per beat ≈ **70 mL/beat**. Formula: $SV = EDV - ESV$.
- **Cardiac Output (CO)** = Volume ejected per minute ≈ **5 L/min**. Formula: $CO = HR \times SV$.
- **Three Determinants of SV**: **Preload**, **Afterload**, **Contractility** — memorize these as the *cardiac triad*.
- **Frank-Starling Law**: Greater preload → greater SV (up to a physiological optimum at ~2.2 μm sarcomere length).
- **Ejection Fraction (EF)** = $\frac{SV}{EDV} \times 100\%$. Normal = **55–70%**. Below 40% = heart failure.
- **Cardiac Index (CI)** = $\frac{CO}{BSA}$. Normal = **2.5–4.0 L/min/m²**.
- **Resting values to memorize**: HR ≈ 70 bpm, EDV ≈ 120 mL, ESV ≈ 50 mL, SV ≈ 70 mL, CO ≈ 5 L/min, EF ≈ 58–60%.
- **Exercise response**: CO can increase **5× to 7×** (athletes up to 35 L/min).
- **Fick Principle alternative**: $CO = \frac{O_2 \text{ consumption}}{Arteriovenous } O_2 \text{ difference}$ — often appears as a Part A bonus question.
- **Clinical link**: Reduced EF → ICD/CRT indication; elevated SV + low HR → athlete's heart; high CO at rest → hyperthyroidism screening.
- **Engineering link**: Bioimpedance cardiography, PPG-based wearable SV estimation, LVAD flow algorithms all derive from these basic principles.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Blood Pressure (BP)

### Formal Definition
**Blood Pressure (BP)** is the lateral pressure exerted by the flowing blood on the walls of the arteries as it is pumped by the heart through the circulatory system. It is a vital hemodynamic parameter that reflects the force the heart must generate to perfuse tissues with oxygen and nutrients.

BP is conventionally measured in millimetres of mercury (**mmHg**) and is expressed as two values:
- **Systolic Blood Pressure (SBP)** — Maximum pressure during ventricular contraction (systole).
- **Diastolic Blood Pressure (DBP)** — Minimum pressure during ventricular relaxation (diastole).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Normal Adult BP (KTU Standard Reference):** 120/80 mmHg
> - Systolic: **120 mmHg**
> - Diastolic: **80 mmHg**
> - Pulse Pressure (PP) = SBP − DBP = **40 mmHg**
> - Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP) ≈ **93 mmHg**

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Garden Hose" Model
Imagine your circulatory system as a **closed garden hose loop** with a water pump (the heart) attached.
- When the pump pushes hard (**systole**), water pressure inside the hose peaks → this is **Systolic BP**.
- When the pump relaxes (**diastole**), the hose walls recoil but pressure doesn't drop to zero because the system is closed → this is **Diastolic BP**.
- If you squeeze the hose (vasoconstriction), pressure rises; if you widen it (vasodilation), pressure falls.
- A taller water tower (greater blood volume) increases the baseline pressure.

This is exactly how BP behaves in arteries: the heart is the pump, arteries are elastic hoses, and total blood volume is the "water in the system."

---

## 1.2 Circulatory System — Overview

### Formal Definition
The **Circulatory System** is a closed-loop network of the heart, blood vessels (arteries, veins, capillaries), and blood, that transports oxygen, nutrients, hormones, metabolic wastes, and heat throughout the body. It is divided into two coupled circuits:
1. **Systemic Circulation** (Greater Circulation)
2. **Pulmonary Circulation** (Lesser Circulation)

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Figure-8" Highway
Think of the cardiovascular system as a **figure-8 racetrack**:
- The heart sits at the crossover (centre of the 8).
- The left side of the 8 = **Systemic Loop** (delivers oxygen to the body).
- The right side of the 8 = **Pulmonary Loop** (refuels blood with oxygen at the lungs).
- Blood circulates continuously, switching loops only at the heart.

---

## 1.3 Systemic Circulation

### Formal Definition
**Systemic Circulation** is the portion of the cardiovascular system that carries **oxygenated blood** from the **left ventricle** of the heart through the **aorta**, distributes it to the body's tissues via arteries, arterioles, and capillaries, and returns **deoxygenated blood** to the **right atrium** via the superior and inferior vena cavae.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Pathway:** Left Ventricle → Aorta → Arteries → Arterioles → Capillaries (gas/nutrient exchange) → Venules → Veins → Vena Cavae → Right Atrium
>
> **Pressure Range:** Aorta ≈ 120/80 mmHg; Capillaries ≈ 30 mmHg; Vena Cavae ≈ 2 mmHg

---

## 1.4 Pulmonary Circulation

### Formal Definition
**Pulmonary Circulation** is the shorter, lower-pressure circuit that carries **deoxygenated blood** from the **right ventricle** to the **lungs** (via the pulmonary arteries) for oxygenation, and returns **oxygenated blood** to the **left atrium** (via the pulmonary veins).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Pathway:** Right Ventricle → Pulmonary Arteries → Lungs (alveolar gas exchange) → Pulmonary Veins → Left Atrium
>
> **Pressure Range:** Pulmonary Artery ≈ 25/8 mmHg (much lower than systemic)

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Local Refuelling Station"
If systemic circulation is the long delivery trip, pulmonary circulation is the **refuelling stop** at a gas station. The truck (deoxygenated blood) makes a short detour to the lungs, picks up oxygen, drops off CO₂, and returns to start the next delivery cycle.

---

## 1.5 Respiratory System

### Formal Definition
The **Respiratory System** is the collection of organs and tissues responsible for **gaseous exchange** between the body and the external environment. It enables intake of **O₂** and expulsion of **CO₂**, while also contributing to acid-base balance, phonation, and thermoregulation.

It is anatomically divided into:
1. **Upper Respiratory Tract** — Nose, nasal cavity, pharynx, larynx (and associated sinuses).
2. **Lower Respiratory Tract** — Trachea, bronchi, bronchioles, alveoli, lungs, and pleura.

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Inverted Tree"
The respiratory tract resembles an **upside-down tree**:
- The **trachea** is the trunk.
- The **two main bronchi** are the first two branches.
- **Bronchioles** are smaller branches.
- **Alveoli** are the leaves, where actual gas exchange (O₂ in, CO₂ out) occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alveoli are the functional unit of respiration** — they are tiny, thin-walled air sacs (≈300 million per lung) surrounded by a dense capillary network where diffusion of gases takes place.

---

## 1.6 Why This Topic Matters for Biomedical Engineers (BME Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU BME Relevance**
> - **Blood Pressure monitoring** → Sphygmomanometers, automated oscillometric devices, wearable cuff-less PPG sensors, implantable pressure sensors.
> - **Circulatory system understanding** → Designing cardiac catheters, stents, ECMO, heart-lung machines, haemodialysis.
> - **Respiratory system understanding** → Ventilator design, pulse oximeters, spirometers, CPAP/BiPAP machines, oxygen concentrators, nebulizers.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Blood Pressure — Detailed Mechanism

### 2.1.1 Determinants of Blood Pressure

Blood pressure is governed by the fundamental hemodynamic relationship:

$$
BP = CO \times SVR
$$

where:
- $BP$ = Blood Pressure (mmHg)
- $CO$ = Cardiac Output (L/min)
- $SVR$ = Systemic Vascular Resistance (mmHg·min/L)

#### Step-by-step Logic of the Determinants
- **Cardiac Output (CO)** = Stroke Volume × Heart Rate. Higher CO → Higher BP.
- **Peripheral Resistance (SVR/TPR)** = Frictional resistance of arterioles. Narrower vessels → Higher SVR → Higher BP.
- **Blood Volume** → More blood volume → Greater venous return → Higher CO → Higher BP.
- **Blood Viscosity** → Thicker blood (e.g., polycythemia) → Higher resistance → Higher BP.
- **Vessel Elasticity** → Loss of elasticity (arteriosclerosis) → Higher SBP, lower DBP, widened PP.

### 2.1.2 Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)

MAP is the **time-averaged arterial pressure** over one cardiac cycle. Because diastole lasts longer than systole physiologically, MAP is not a simple arithmetic mean.

$$
MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
MAP = \frac{2(DBP) + SBP}{3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Significance of MAP:** MAP must remain ≥ 60 mmHg to ensure adequate perfusion of vital organs (brain, kidneys, heart). Below 60 mmHg → tissue ischemia.

### 2.1.3 Pulse Pressure (PP)

$$
PP = SBP - DBP
$$

- **Normal PP** ≈ 40 mmHg.
- **Widened PP** (>60 mmHg) → Aortic regurgitation, arteriosclerosis, hyperthyroidism.
- **Narrowed PP** (<25 mmHg) → Cardiac tamponade, severe heart failure, aortic stenosis.

### 2.1.4 Regulation of Blood Pressure

BP is regulated by two main mechanisms:

#### A. Short-Term Regulation (Neural) — seconds to minutes
- **Baroreceptor Reflex:** Stretch receptors in the carotid sinus and aortic arch.
  - ↑BP → Baroreceptors fire faster → Signals to medulla → ↑ Parasympathetic (vagus) output → ↓ HR, ↓ contractility → BP falls.
  - ↓BP → Opposite effect → Sympathetic activation → ↑ HR, ↑ vasoconstriction → BP rises.
- **Chemoreceptor Reflex:** Located in carotid and aortic bodies; respond to ↓O₂, ↑CO₂, ↓pH → trigger sympathetic response.
- **CNS Ischemic Response:** Severe brain ischemia → massive sympathetic outflow → ↑BP.

#### B. Long-Term Regulation (Hormonal/Renal) — hours to days
- **Renin-Angiotensin-Aldosterone System (RAAS):**
  - ↓Renal perfusion → Juxtaglomerular cells release **Renin** → Renin converts Angiotensinogen → Angiotensin I → (ACE in lungs) → Angiotensin II.
  - Angiotensin II is a potent **vasoconstrictor** → ↑SVR → ↑BP.
  - Also stimulates **Aldosterone** release from adrenal cortex → Na⁺ and water retention → ↑Blood Volume → ↑BP.
- **ADH (Vasopressin):** Released from posterior pituitary; promotes water reabsorption → ↑Blood Volume → ↑BP.
- **Atrial Natriuretic Peptide (ANP):** Released from atria when stretched (↑volume); promotes Na⁺ and water excretion → ↓Blood Volume → ↓BP.

---

## 2.2 Systemic Circulation — Detailed Pathway

### 2.2.1 Order of Blood Flow
**Left Ventricle → Aorta → Arteries → Arterioles → Capillaries → Venules → Veins → Superior/Inferior Vena Cava → Right Atrium**

### 2.2.2 Key Functions
- Delivers O₂ and nutrients to body tissues.
- Removes CO₂ and metabolic waste (via kidneys, liver, skin).
- Distributes hormones from endocrine glands.
- Regulates body temperature (cutaneous vasodilation/constriction).
- Maintains fluid and acid-base balance.

### 2.2.3 Pressure Gradient in Systemic Circulation

| Segment | Approx. Pressure (mmHg) |
|---|---|
| Aorta (peak systolic) | **120** |
| Aorta (diastolic) | **80** |
| Small arteries | 70–90 |
| Arterioles | 35–70 |
| Capillaries (arterial end) | 30–35 |
| Capillaries (venous end) | 12–15 |
| Venules | 10–15 |
| Veins | 5–10 |
| Vena Cavae (right atrium) | 2–5 |

> [!NOTE]
> The progressive pressure drop from **120 mmHg (aorta) to ~2 mmHg (right atrium)** is the driving force for blood flow through the systemic circuit. This is an application of the **Bernoulli principle** for closed-loop fluid flow.

### 2.2.4 Major Systemic Arteries and Their Distributions
- **Aorta** → Ascending, Arch, Descending (Thoracic + Abdominal)
- **Carotid arteries** → Head and brain
- **Subclavian arteries** → Upper limbs
- **Renal arteries** → Kidneys
- **Mesenteric arteries** → GI tract
- **Iliac arteries** → Lower limbs
- **Coronary arteries** → Heart muscle

---

## 2.3 Pulmonary Circulation — Detailed Pathway

### 2.3.1 Order of Blood Flow
**Right Ventricle → Pulmonary Trunk → Right and Left Pulmonary Arteries → Lobar Arteries → Arterioles → Alveolar Capillaries → Venules → Pulmonary Veins → Left Atrium**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pulmonary Arteries carry DEOXYGENATED blood**, while **Pulmonary Veins carry OXYGENATED blood** — this is the **opposite** of systemic vessels, a common point of confusion in KTU exams.

### 2.3.2 Key Functions
- Oxygenates blood (O₂ loading at alveoli).
- Removes CO₂ (CO₂ unloading at alveoli).
- Filters blood (small emboli trapped in lung capillaries).
- Acts as a **reservoir** for blood (~9% of total blood volume).

### 2.3.3 Pressure Characteristics
- Pulmonary circulation is a **low-pressure, low-resistance** system.
- **Normal Pulmonary Artery Pressure:** 25/8 mmHg (mean ≈ 15 mmHg).
- This is much lower than systemic (120/80 mmHg) because:
  - Pulmonary vessels are shorter and more distensible.
  - The right ventricle generates less force than the left.

### 2.3.4 Comparison Table — Systemic vs Pulmonary Circulation

| Parameter | Systemic | Pulmonary |
|---|---|---|
| **Side of heart** | Left | Right |
| **Blood type pumped** | Oxygenated | Deoxygenated |
| **Vessel type** | Arteries = O₂; Veins = de-O₂ | Arteries = de-O₂; Veins = O₂ |
| **Pressure (mmHg)** | 120/80 | 25/8 |
| **Resistance** | High | Low |
| **Path length** | Long | Short |
| **Function** | Nutrient/waste exchange | Gas exchange only |
| **% Blood volume** | ~84% | ~9% |

---

## 2.4 Respiratory System — Detailed Structural Organization

### 2.4.1 Upper Respiratory Tract

#### (A) Nose and Nasal Cavity
- **External nares** (nostrils) → entry point for air.
- **Nasal cavity** lined with **pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium** with goblet cells.
- Functions: warms, humidifies, and filters inspired air.
- **Conchae (turbinates)** increase surface area and create turbulent airflow.

#### (B) Paranasal Sinuses
- **Frontal, Maxillary, Ethmoidal, Sphenoidal sinuses.**
- Lighten the skull, produce mucus, resonate voice.

#### (C) Pharynx (Throat)
- **Nasopharynx:** Behind nasal cavity; contains pharyngeal tonsil (adenoids).
- **Oropharynx:** Behind oral cavity; contains palatine and lingual tonsils.
- **Laryngopharynx:** Continues into oesophagus and larynx.

#### (D) Larynx (Voice Box)
- Cartilages: **Thyroid, Cricoid, Arytenoid, Epiglottis, Corniculate, Cuneiform.**
- Contains **vocal cords** (vocal folds) responsible for phonation.
- **Epiglottis** is a leaf-shaped flap that closes the glottis during swallowing to prevent aspiration.

### 2.4.2 Lower Respiratory Tract

#### (A) Trachea (Windpipe)
- ~12 cm long, 2.5 cm diameter.
- Composed of **15–20 C-shaped hyaline cartilage rings** (open posteriorly, where the oesophagus lies).
- Lined with ciliated pseudostratified columnar epithelium.
- Bifurcates at the **carina** (T4–T5 vertebral level) into two main bronchi.

#### (B) Bronchi
- **Right main bronchus:** Wider, shorter, more vertical → foreign bodies often lodge here.
- **Left main bronchus:** Narrower, longer, more oblique.
- Branch into **lobar (secondary) bronchi** (3 right, 2 left), then **segmental (tertiary) bronchi**.

#### (C) Bronchioles
- Diameter < 1 mm; **no cartilage**; surrounded by **smooth muscle**.
- Regulated by the **Autonomic Nervous System**:
  - **Parasympathetic (ACh)** → Bronchoconstriction.
  - **Sympathetic (β₂)** → Bronchodilation (salbutamol mimics this).
- **Terminal bronchioles** → end of conducting zone.
- **Respiratory bronchioles** → start of respiratory zone; have alveoli budding off their walls.

#### (D) Alveoli
- ~300 million per lung; total surface area ≈ **70 m²** (≈ half a tennis court).
- Lined with two types of cells:
  - **Type I Pneumocytes** (95% surface) — thin, squamous, gas exchange.
  - **Type II Pneumocytes** (5% surface) — produce **pulmonary surfactant** (reduces surface tension).
- **Alveolar Macrophages (Dust Cells)** — phagocytose inhaled debris.

#### (E) Lungs
- **Right lung:** 3 lobes (superior, middle, inferior); 10 segments.
- **Left lung:** 2 lobes (superior, inferior); 8 segments; cardiac notch.
- Covered by **Pleura** — two layers: **Visceral pleura** (adheres to lung) and **Parietal pleura** (lines thoracic cavity); **Pleural cavity** between them contains serous fluid for frictionless movement.

#### (F) Diaphragm
- Dome-shaped skeletal muscle; **primary muscle of inspiration**.
- Innervated by the **Phrenic nerve** (C3–C5).
- During inspiration, it contracts and flattens → ↑ thoracic volume → ↓ intrapleural pressure → air rushes in.

### 2.4.3 Functional Zones of the Respiratory System

| Zone | Structures | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Conducting Zone** | Nose → Terminal Bronchioles | Air conduction only (no gas exchange) — **Anatomical Dead Space (~150 mL)** |
| **Respiratory Zone** | Respiratory Bronchioles → Alveolar Ducts → Alveolar Sacs → Alveoli | Actual gas exchange |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas for the KTU Module 3 portion of PCBRT205.**

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $BP = CO \times SVR$ | Blood pressure = cardiac output × systemic vascular resistance | mmHg |
| 2 | $CO = SV \times HR$ | Cardiac output = stroke volume × heart rate | L/min |
| 3 | $MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)$ | Mean arterial pressure | mmHg |
| 4 | $MAP = \frac{2(DBP) + SBP}{3}$ | Alternate MAP form | mmHg |
| 5 | $PP = SBP - DBP$ | Pulse pressure | mmHg |
| 6 | $Q = \frac{\Delta P}{R}$ | Ohm's law for fluid flow (flow = pressure gradient / resistance) | L/min |
| 7 | $R = \frac{8\eta L}{\pi r^{4}}$ | Poiseuille's law (resistance inversely ∝ r⁴) | mmHg·min/mL |
| 8 | $V = IR$ (Ohm's law analogy) | Voltage = Current × Resistance — analogous to ΔP = Q × R | — |
| 9 | $TLC = TV + IRV + ERV + RV$ | Total lung capacity = sum of all 4 lung volumes | mL |
| 10 | $VC = TV + IRV + ERV$ | Vital capacity | mL |
| 11 | $IC = TV + IRV$ | Inspiratory capacity | mL |
| 12 | $FRC = ERV + RV$ | Functional residual capacity | mL |
| 13 | $MVV = f_{max} \times V_{max}$ | Maximal voluntary ventilation | L/min |
| 14 | $FEF_{25-75} = \frac{FVC_{25\%} - FVC_{75\%}}{t_{75\%} - t_{25\%}}$ | Mid-expiratory flow rate | L/s |
| 15 | $FEV_{1}/FVC$ ratio | Tiffeneau-Pinelli index (≥ 80% = normal) | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BME Engineering Utility:**
> - **Poiseuille's Law (R ∝ 1/r⁴):** Atherosclerotic plaques that reduce vessel radius by 50% increase resistance by **16×** — directly relevant to **stent design** and **flow modelling**.
> - **Lung volumes & capacities:** Essential for interpreting **spirometer output** in pulmonary function tests (PFTs), the basis for **ventilator and CPAP machine calibration**.

---

| Biomedical Application | Underlying Physiological Concept |

| **Sphygmomanometer / Digital BP monitor** | Korotkoff sounds, oscillometric method |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)

### Step 1: Conceptual Foundation
Arterial pressure varies between systolic ($SBP$) and diastolic ($DBP$) during one cardiac cycle. We seek a single "time-averaged" value that represents the mean pressure pushing blood through the arteries.

### Step 2: Cardiac Cycle Time Analysis
In one cardiac cycle:
- **Systole duration** ≈ $1/3$ of cycle
- **Diastole duration** ≈ $2/3$ of cycle

> This is because at a typical heart rate of 75 bpm, systole lasts ~0.3 s and diastole lasts ~0.5 s.

### Step 3: Compute Time-Averaged Pressure
Mean arterial pressure is the integral of pressure over the cardiac cycle, divided by the cycle period. Approximating the arterial waveform as a piecewise linear function:

$$
MAP = \frac{1}{T_{cycle}} \int_{0}^{T_{cycle}} P(t)\, dt
$$

Breaking the integral into systole and diastole:

$$
MAP = \frac{1}{T} \left[ \int_{0}^{T_{sys}} P_{sys}(t)\, dt + \int_{T_{sys}}^{T} P_{dia}(t)\, dt \right]
$$

### Step 4: Approximate Each Portion
- During systole, the area under the curve is approximately the average of SBP and DBP, weighted by systolic time: 
  - $\int P_{sys} \approx \frac{SBP + DBP}{2} \times T_{sys}$
- During diastole, pressure decays roughly exponentially from SBP to DBP; area ≈ $\frac{SBP + DBP}{2} \times T_{dia}$ (linear approximation).

### Step 5: Apply Time Weights (1/3 systole, 2/3 diastole)

$$
MAP = \frac{1}{T} \left[ \frac{SBP + DBP}{2} \cdot \frac{T}{3} + \frac{SBP + DBP}{2} \cdot \frac{2T}{3} \right]
$$

Wait — this gives the arithmetic mean. We need a more careful treatment: the actual average during systole and diastole differs.

### Step 6: Corrected Physiological Model
In reality, mean pressure during systole is approximately the **midpoint** between SBP and the diastolic-ascending baseline, and during diastole, the exponential decay gives a mean closer to $\frac{SBP + DBP}{2}$ as well. The empirically validated weighting is:

$$
MAP = \frac{1}{3}SBP + \frac{2}{3}DBP
$$

### Step 7: Rearranged Form
Rearranging the above to express MAP in terms of DBP and pulse pressure ($PP = SBP - DBP$):

$$
MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP)
$$

$$
\therefore \quad MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}\, PP
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MAP = DBP + ⅓ (SBP − DBP)** is the **standard KTU exam formula** for mean arterial pressure.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Example: MAP Calculation

**Problem:** A patient has SBP = 130 mmHg and DBP = 85 mmHg. Calculate MAP and PP.

**Solution:**

$$
PP = SBP - DBP = 130 - 85 = 45\ \text{mmHg}
$$

$$
MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}(SBP - DBP) = 85 + \frac{1}{3}(45) = 85 + 15 = 100\ \text{mmHg}
$$

**Answer:** PP = **45 mmHg**, MAP = **100 mmHg** (normal range).

---

## 3.3 Worked Problem — Cardiac Output and BP Relationship

**Problem:** A patient has a heart rate of 80 bpm and a stroke volume of 70 mL. If systemic vascular resistance is 20 mmHg·min/L, calculate:
1. Cardiac Output (CO)
2. Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)
3. Pulse Pressure (PP) given DBP = 80 mmHg

**Step-by-step Solution:**

#### Part 1: Cardiac Output

$$
CO = SV \times HR = 70\ \text{mL} \times 80\ \text{bpm} = 5600\ \text{mL/min} = 5.6\ \text{L/min}
$$

#### Part 2: Mean Arterial Pressure

$$
BP = CO \times SVR
$$

$$
MAP = 5.6\ \text{L/min} \times 20\ \frac{\text{mmHg·min}}{\text{L}} = 112\ \text{mmHg}
$$

#### Part 3: Pulse Pressure
We have $MAP = 112$ mmHg and $DBP = 80$ mmHg. From the MAP formula:

$$
MAP = DBP + \frac{1}{3}\,PP
$$

$$
112 = 80 + \frac{1}{3}\, PP
$$

$$
\frac{1}{3}\, PP = 32 \quad \Rightarrow \quad PP = 96\ \text{mmHg}
$$

Wait — this is unusually high. This suggests the case parameters are not internally consistent; a typical PP is 30–50 mmHg. The example is illustrative only.

> [!NOTE]
> In a **real clinical scenario**, BP is measured first, then SVR is back-calculated. The above numerical exercise is for testing the formula relationships, not for a realistic case.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Poiseuille's Law (Resistance to Flow)

### Step 1: Start from Basic Fluid Dynamics
For laminar flow of a viscous fluid through a cylindrical tube, the volumetric flow rate ($Q$) is given by the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation**:

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P\, r^{4}}{8 \eta L}
$$

where:
- $\Delta P$ = Pressure gradient along the tube (Pa or mmHg)
- $r$ = radius of the tube
- $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity of the fluid
- $L$ = length of the tube

### Step 2: Solve for Resistance
Rearranging to isolate $\Delta P$:

$$
\Delta P = Q \times \frac{8\eta L}{\pi r^{4}}
$$

Comparing with the electrical Ohm's law form $\Delta P = Q \times R$:

$$
R = \frac{8\eta L}{\pi r^{4}}
$$

### Step 3: Key Insight — Fourth-Power Dependence
Since $R \propto 1/r^{4}$, **small changes in vessel radius have enormous effects on resistance**.

**Numerical demonstration:** If radius is reduced to half ($r \rightarrow r/2$):

$$
R_{new} = \frac{8\eta L}{\pi (r/2)^{4}} = \frac{8\eta L}{\pi r^{4}/16} = 16 \cdot R_{old}
$$

So **halving** the radius increases resistance **16-fold**!

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clinical / BME Takeaway:** A 50% stenosis in a coronary artery (radius reduced by ½) increases resistance 16×, dramatically reducing flow. This is why **angioplasty** and **stent placement** to restore vessel radius are so clinically effective.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Lung Volumes and Capacities

**Worked Example:** Given the following lung volumes for a healthy adult male:
- Tidal Volume (TV) = 500 mL
- Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV) = 3000 mL
- Expiratory Reserve Volume (ERV) = 1100 mL
- Residual Volume (RV) = 1200 mL

**Compute all four lung capacities.**

### Step 1: Inspiratory Capacity (IC)

$$
IC = TV + IRV = 500 + 3000 = 3500\ \text{mL}
$$

### Step 2: Functional Residual Capacity (FRC)

$$
FRC = ERV + RV = 1100 + 1200 = 2300\ \text{mL}
$$

### Step 3: Vital Capacity (VC)

$$
VC = TV + IRV + ERV = 500 + 3000 + 1100 = 4600\ \text{mL}
$$

### Step 4: Total Lung Capacity (TLC)

$$
TLC = TV + IRV + ERV + RV = 500 + 3000 + 1100 + 1200 = 5800\ \text{mL}
$$

### Summary Table

| Capacity | Value (mL) | Clinical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **IC** | **3500** | Maximum air inhaled after a normal expiration |
| **FRC** | **2300** | Volume remaining after normal expiration; oxygen reservoir |
| **VC** | **4600** | Maximal air exhaled after maximal inspiration; reduced in restrictive disease |
| **TLC** | **5800** | Total lung size; increased in emphysema, decreased in fibrosis |

---

## 3.6 Spirometry — FEV₁/FVC Calculation

**Problem:** A patient's spirometry shows:
- FVC (Forced Vital Capacity) = 4.0 L
- FEV₁ (Forced Expiratory Volume in 1 s) = 3.2 L

Calculate the FEV₁/FVC ratio and interpret the result.

### Step 1: Compute the Ratio

$$
\frac{FEV_{1}}{FVC} = \frac{3.2\ \text{L}}{4.0\ \text{L}} = 0.80 = 80\%
$$

### Step 2: Interpret
- **Normal:** ≥ 80% (this patient).
- **Obstructive disease (asthma, COPD):** < 70% (airflow limitation).
- **Restrictive disease (fibrosis):** ≥ 80% (or higher), but reduced absolute FVC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tiffeneau Index = FEV₁/FVC** is a **must-know** for KTU BME exams when discussing respiratory system diagnostics and ventilator/sensor design.
