Standard value = **500 mL**. **(b) Functional Residual Capacity (FRC):** The volume of air remaining in the lungs at the **end of a normal (passive) expiration**. It is the sum of Expiratory Reserve Volume (ERV) and Residual Volume (RV). Standard value = **2400 mL**. **(c) Dead Space (VD):** The volume of air in the **respiratory tract that does not participate in gas exchange**. Includes the conducting zone (trachea, bronchi, bronchioles, terminal bronchioles). Standard anatomical value = **150 mL**. --- ### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **State Boyle's Law. How is it applied in the measurement of Functional Residual Capacity using a body plethysmograph?** **Model Answer:** **Boyle's Law:** At constant temperature, the pressure of a gas is inversely proportional to its volume. $$P_1 \cdot V_1 = P_2 \cdot V_2$$ **Application in Body Plethysmograph:** The subject sits inside a sealed chamber of known volume. The subject breathes through a mouthpiece connected to a shutter. When the shutter closes at end-expiration, the subject makes breathing efforts against it. The **change in alveolar pressure** is measured, and the **change in box volume** (and hence box pressure) is measured simultaneously. Applying Boyle's Law to the lungs and the box separately allows calculation of the **thoracic gas volume**, which equals the **FRC**. $$\boxed{FRC = V_{thoracic\ gas} = \frac{\Delta V_{box}}{\Delta P_{alv}} \times P_{atm}}$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **mechanics of inspiration and expiration** with the help of **pressure-volume relationships** in the lungs. Include a clear description of the role of the **diaphragm, intercostal muscles, and intrapleural pressure**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Define **lung compliance**. Discuss the **clinical significance** of altered compliance in **emphysema** and **pulmonary fibrosis**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A(a): **Step 1 — Pre-inspiration State (FRC):** - Diaphragm is relaxed and dome-shaped. - Intrapleural pressure (Pip) = **–4 mmHg** (sub-atmospheric). - Alveolar pressure (Palv) = **0 mmHg** (equal to atmospheric). - No airflow. - Lung volume = **2400 mL** (FRC). **Step 2 — Initiation of Inspiration:** - **Phrenic nerve** stimulates the diaphragm to contract. - Diaphragm flattens, increasing the **vertical dimension** of the thoracic cavity. - **External intercostal muscles** contract, elevating the ribs (pump-handle movement in upper ribs, bucket-handle movement in lower ribs), increasing the **anteroposterior and transverse diameters**. **Step 3 — Pressure Changes During Inspiration:** - Thoracic volume ↑. - Intrapleural pressure becomes more negative: from **–4 mmHg to –8 mmHg**. - This increase in negativity expands the lungs outward. - Alveolar volume ↑, so by **Boyle's Law**, **alveolar pressure drops to –1 mmHg**. - **Pressure gradient: Patm (0) > Palv (–1)** → Air flows INTO the lungs. - Approximately **500 mL** of air enters (tidal volume). **Step 4 — End of Inspiration:** - Diaphragm is fully contracted. - Palv returns to **0 mmHg** (pressure equilibrium). - Airflow stops momentarily. - Lung volume reaches ~ **2900 mL**. **Step 5 — Expiration (Passive):** - Diaphragm and external intercostals **relax**. - **Elastic recoil** of the lungs and chest wall pushes air out. - Lung volume ↓ → Alveolar pressure rises to **+1 mmHg**. - **Pressure gradient: Palv (+1) > Patm (0)** → Air flows OUT. - ~ 500 mL expelled. **Step 6 — Forced Expiration (Active):** - **Internal intercostals** contract → ribs pulled downward and inward. - **Abdominal muscles** contract → increase intra-abdominal pressure → push diaphragm upward. - Palv can rise to **+20 to +40 mmHg** during coughing. - Increased airflow for speech, coughing, exercise. **Summary Table (Mandatory in KTU Answers):** | Phase | Diaphragm | Pip (mmHg) | Palv (mmHg) | Airflow | |---|---|---|---|---| | Pre-inspiration | Relaxed | –4 | 0 | None | | Mid-inspiration | Contracting | –6 | –1 | Inward | | End-inspiration | Contracted | –8 | 0 | None | | Mid-expiration | Relaxing | –6 | +1 | Outward | | End-expiration | Relaxed | –4 | 0 | None | --- #### Model Solution to Question A(b): **Definition of Lung Compliance:** Lung compliance (CL) is defined as the **change in lung volume per unit change in transpulmonary pressure**. $$C_L = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta P_{tp}} = \frac{\Delta V}{P_{alv} - P_{ip}}$$ - **Normal value:** 200 mL/cmH₂O (0.2 L/cmH₂O) - **High compliance** → Easy to inflate, but lungs collapse easily (loss of elastic recoil). - **Low compliance** → Stiff lungs, require more pressure to inflate (more work of breathing). **Emphysema (↑ Compliance):** - **Pathophysiology:** Destruction of alveolar walls and loss of **elastin** and **collagen** fibres (often due to smoking). - **Effect on Compliance:** Lung compliance **increases** (lungs become floppy / over-distensible). - **P-V Curve:** Shifts **leftward and upward**, steep slope. - **Clinical Consequences:** - Increased FRC, increased TLC. - Decreased elastic recoil → difficulty in expiration → **air trapping**. - Reduced alveolar surface area → **impaired gas exchange** → dyspnoea. - Increased **FEV1/FVC ratio interpretation** depends on FVC drop. **Pulmonary Fibrosis (↓ Compliance):** - **Pathophysiology:** Thickening and scarring of the interstitium of the lung (idiopathic, occupational exposure, autoimmune). - **Effect on Compliance:** Lung compliance **decreases dramatically** (stiff lungs). - **P-V Curve:** Shifts **rightward and downward**, flattened slope. - **Clinical Consequences:** - Reduced lung volumes (↓ VC, ↓ TLC, ↓ FRC). - Increased work of breathing → patient breathes with **rapid, shallow breaths** to minimize effort. - Impaired diffusion across thickened alveolar-capillary membrane → **hypoxaemia**. - Spirometry: **Normal or increased FEV1/FVC ratio** (restrictive pattern). **Comparative Summary Table:** | Parameter | Emphysema | Pulmonary Fibrosis | |---|---|---| | Compliance | ↑ High | ↓ Low | | Elastic Recoil | ↓ Lost | ↑ Increased | | FRC | ↑ Increased | ↓ Decreased | | TLC | ↑ Increased | ↓ Decreased | | Work of Breathing | ↑ (esp. expiration) | ↑ (esp. inspiration) | | FEV1/FVC | Decreased | Normal/Increased | | Pattern | Obstructive | Restrictive | --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** State and explain **Poiseuille's Law** as applied to airflow in the respiratory tract. Discuss how the **radius of the bronchioles** affects airway resistance. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe the role of **pulmonary surfactant** in alveolar mechanics. Explain **Laplace's Law** and the consequences of surfactant deficiency in **neonatal respiratory distress syndrome (NRDS)**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B(a): **Statement of Poiseuille's Law:** For laminar flow of a fluid through a cylindrical tube, the volumetric flow rate ($\dot{V}$) is: $$\dot{V} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot \pi \cdot r^4}{8 \cdot \eta \cdot L}$$ Where: - $\Delta P$ = Pressure gradient across the tube (cmH₂O or Pa) - $r$ = Radius of the tube (cm) - $\eta$ = Dynamic viscosity of the fluid (poise) - $L$ = Length of the tube (cm) **Application to Respiratory System:** - Trachea, bronchi, and bronchioles act as a series of branching tubes carrying air (a Newtonian fluid for our purposes). - Airflow is **laminar** in small airways and **turbulent** in large airways (trachea, main bronchi) during rapid breathing. **Derivation of Airway Resistance:** $$R_{aw} = \frac{\Delta P}{\dot{V}} = \frac{8 \eta L}{\pi r^4}$$ $$\boxed{R_{aw} \propto \frac{1}{r^4}}$$ **Effect of Bronchiolar Radius — The r⁴ Phenomenon:** | Bronchiolar Radius Change | Airway Resistance Change | Airflow Change | |---|---|---| | Normal (r = 1.0 unit) | 1.0 (baseline) | 100% | | 20% decrease (r = 0.8) | 2.44× baseline | 41% | | 50% decrease (r = 0.5) | 16× baseline | 6.25% | | 20% increase (r = 1.2) | 0.48× baseline | 208% | **Clinical Implications:** 1. **Asthma:** Bronchoconstriction due to smooth muscle spasm, mucosal oedema, and mucus hypersecretion. Even a small reduction in radius causes **massive** increase in resistance and severe dyspnoea. 2. **COPD (Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease):** Chronic inflammation narrows small airways, increasing Raw. 3. **Bronchodilators (Salbutamol, Ipratropium):** Act on β₂-adrenergic receptors to relax smooth muscle → increase r → decrease Raw. 4. **Heliox therapy:** A mixture of **Helium (low density) and Oxygen** reduces turbulent flow resistance, used in severe airway obstruction. > [!NOTE] > KTU Board examiners frequently ask: "Why does bronchoconstriction cause such severe breathlessness?" The answer must always reference the **r⁴ relationship** from Poiseuille's Law. --- #### Model Solution to Question B(b): **Role of Pulmonary Surfactant:** Pulmonary surfactant is a **lipoprotein complex** (primarily **dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine - DPPC**) secreted by **Type II pneumocytes (alveolar cells)** lining the alveoli. **Functions:** 1. **Reduces surface tension** at the air-liquid interface in the alveoli. 2. **Increases lung compliance** → reduces the work of breathing. 3. **Stabilizes alveoli** of different sizes (prevents small alveoli from collapsing into large ones). 4. **Reduces the tendency for pulmonary oedema** by reducing the suction force on the alveolar capillaries. **Laplace's Law (Alveolar Sphere):** For a spherical bubble, the pressure difference across the wall is: $$\Delta P = \frac{2T}{r}$$ Where: - $\Delta P$ = Trans-alveolar pressure - $T$ = Surface tension - $r$ = Radius of the alveolus **Key Problem Without Surfactant:** If surface tension is constant (like pure water, T = 70 dynes/cm), then **smaller alveoli have higher collapsing pressure** than larger ones. This would cause: - Small alveoli to **empty into large alveoli** (instability). - Progressive **alveolar collapse** (atelectasis). - Massive increase in work of breathing. **How Surfactant Solves This:** Surfactant **reduces surface tension more in smaller alveoli** (and less in larger alveoli), equalizing the pressure across alveoli of different sizes. This is known as the **"variable surface tension"** property. | Alveolar Condition | Surface Tension Effect | Result | |---|---|---| | Large alveolus (r large) | T relatively higher | ΔP normalized | | Small alveolus (r small) | T greatly reduced | ΔP normalized → stability | **Numerical Comparison (as derived in Section 3.1):** - Without surfactant: ΔP ≈ **14 mmHg** (alveolus would collapse) - With surfactant: ΔP ≈ **5 mmHg** (stable, easily held open) **Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome (NRDS):** **Pathophysiology:** - Occurs primarily in **premature infants** (gestational age < 35 weeks). - **Type II pneumocytes are immature** → insufficient surfactant production. - Surfactant is normally produced after **24–28 weeks** of gestation and reaches mature levels by **35 weeks**. **Consequences:** 1. **High alveolar surface tension** → high collapsing pressure. 2. **Diffuse atelectasis** (alveolar collapse) → ↓ lung compliance. 3. **Severe hypoxaemia** and **hypercapnia**. 4. **Increased work of breathing** → respiratory muscle fatigue. 5. Hyaline membrane formation in alveoli (from leaked plasma proteins). **Clinical Features (Downes Score):** - Tachypnoea (> 60/min) - Retractions (intercostal, subcostal) - Grunting (infant attempts to maintain positive end-expiratory pressure) - Cyanosis - Nasal flaring **Treatment:** 1. **Exogenous surfactant therapy** (e.g., Beractant, Poractant alfa) via endotracheal tube. 2. **CPAP / Mechanical ventilation** with PEEP to keep alveoli open. 3. **Antenatal corticosteroids** (Betamethasone) to mother — accelerates fetal lung maturity. 4. **Oxygen therapy** with careful titration (avoid retinopathy of prematurity and oxygen toxicity). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:** > 1. **Confusing Intrapleural Pressure with Alveolar Pressure.** Intrapleural pressure is ALWAYS negative (during normal breathing); alveolar pressure fluctuates above and below atmospheric. > 2. **Forgetting units in compliance calculations.** Always specify L/cmH₂O or mL/cmH₂O. > 3. **Stating "Boyle's Law applies to the whole respiratory system"** — be precise: it applies to the alveolar gas. > 4. **In surfactant-related questions**, always mention **Type II pneumocytes** and **DPPC** (the main phospholipid) for full marks. > 5. **In NRDS questions**, mention the **gestational age threshold (~35 weeks)** and the role of **antenatal steroids** — examiners specifically look for this clinical correlation. > 6. **Do not write "diaphragm goes up during inspiration"** — the diaphragm moves **DOWN** (flattens) during inspiration. > 7. **For Poiseuille's Law**, do not forget the **r⁴ dependence** — this is the most-marks-bearing point. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mechanics of Respiration** ### Key Definitions - ☐ **Tidal Volume (VT)** = 500 mL - ☐ **Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV)** = 3000 mL - ☐ **Expiratory Reserve Volume (ERV)** = 1100 mL - ☐ **Residual Volume (RV)** = 1200 mL - ☐ **FRC** = ERV + RV = 2400 mL - ☐ **Vital Capacity (VC)** = IRV + VT + ERV = 4600 mL - ☐ **Total Lung Capacity (TLC)** = VC + RV = 5800 mL - ☐ **Anatomical Dead Space** = 150 mL (or 1 mL/lb body weight) ### Pressure Benchmarks - ☐ Atmospheric Pressure = **760 mmHg = 1 atm = 101.325 kPa** - ☐ Intrapleural Pressure at FRC = **–4 mmHg** - ☐ Intrapleural Pressure at end-inspiration = **–8 mmHg** - ☐ Alveolar Pressure at FRC = **0 mmHg** - ☐ Alveolar Pressure during inspiration = **–1 mmHg** - ☐ Alveolar Pressure during expiration = **+1 mmHg** - ☐ Transpulmonary Pressure = **Palv – Pip** ### Essential Laws - ☐ **Boyle's Law:** $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ (at constant T) - ☐ **Hooke's Law:** $P_{elastic} = \Delta V / C_L$ - ☐ **Poiseuille's Law:** $\dot{V} = \Delta P \pi r^4 / (8 \eta L)$ — **flow ∝ r⁴** - ☐ **Laplace's Law:** $\Delta P = 2T / r$ — **surface tension effect** - ☐ **Fick's Law of Diffusion:** (links to gas exchange) ### Muscles of Respiration - ☐ **Inspiration (quiet):** Diaphragm (primary), External intercostals - ☐ **Inspiration (forced):** Sternocleidomastoid, Scalenes, Pectoralis minor - ☐ **Expiration (quiet):** Passive (elastic recoil) - ☐ **Expiration (forced):** Internal intercostals, Abdominal muscles (rectus abdominis, external/internal obliques, transversus) ### Compliance Values - ☐ **Normal Lung Compliance:** 0.2 L/cmH₂O - ☐ **Normal Chest Wall Compliance:** 0.2 L/cmH₂O - ☐ **Total Respiratory Compliance:** 0.1 L/cmH₂O - ☐ **Normal Airway Resistance:** 0.5–2.5 cmH₂O/L/s ### Clinical Correlations - ☐ **Emphysema:** ↑ Compliance, ↓ Elastic recoil, obstructive pattern - ☐ **Pulmonary Fibrosis:** ↓ Compliance, restrictive pattern - ☐ **Asthma:** ↑↑ Airway resistance (r⁴ effect) - ☐ **NRDS:** Surfactant deficiency, premature infants, treated with exogenous surfactant + CPAP - ☐ **Pneumothorax:** Loss of negative intrapleural pressure → lung collapse ### KTU Exam Quick-Fire Formulas - ☐ Minute Ventilation = $V_T \times RR$ - ☐ Alveolar Ventilation = $(V_T - V_D) \times RR$ - ☐ FEV1/FVC Ratio ≥ 0.8 = normal; < 0.7 = obstructive - ☐ Work of Breathing = $\int P \, dV$ ≈ 0.5 J/breath at rest; ↑ 10× in heavy exercise - ☐ **NEVER forget: Ventilation equation, Compliance equation, Poiseuille's r⁴ — these are the three highest-yield formulas.** --- > **End of Notes — Mechanics of Respiration | Module 3 | PCBRT205** > *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Protocol — 2024 Scheme*

topics.title: Gaseous Exchange
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBRT205 - ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING (BIOMEDICAL AND ROBOTIC ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Gaseous Exchange # Module 3 — Gaseous Exchange ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Gaseous Exchange (External Respiration)** is the biophysical process by which oxygen ($O_2$) is transferred from the atmospheric air into the pulmonary capillaries, and carbon dioxide ($CO_2$) is simultaneously transferred from the pulmonary capillaries into the alveoli, driven entirely by **partial pressure gradients** across the respiratory membrane (alveolar–capillary barrier) of thickness approximately **0.6 µm**. In strict KTU 2024 terminology, the full respiratory cycle is partitioned into four sequential phases: 1. **Pulmonary Ventilation** — Bulk movement of air into and out of the lungs. 2. **External Respiration** — Diffusion of gases between alveoli and pulmonary capillary blood. 3. **Transport of Gases** — Bulk movement of $O_2$ and $CO_2$ via blood to/from tissues. 4. **Internal Respiration** — Diffusion of gases between systemic capillaries and tissue cells. > [!NOTE] > **Cellular Respiration** (the mitochondrial generation of ATP from glucose) is a *separate biochemical* process and is **not** the same as gaseous exchange, although it provides the metabolic *demand* that drives it. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Factory Pneumatic System" Imagine a large industrial factory (your body) that: - Has a loading dock (the **alveoli**) where raw material trucks ($O_2$ molecules) arrive, - Sends the material through a network of conveyor belts (**bloodstream**), - Delivers it to individual work-stations (**tissue cells**), - And ships the waste product ($CO_2$) back to a separate exhaust port (the **lungs**) for release. The "trucks" do not magically appear at the loading dock — they are *pumped* in by bellows (the **diaphragm + intercostal muscles**), and they move between the dock and the trucks only because of a **pressure difference** (the partial pressure gradient). If the pressure equalizes, exchange stops. The loading dock walls (**respiratory membrane**) are kept extremely thin (≈ 0.6 µm) so diffusion is fast. ### 1.3 Engineering Relevance For a biomedical/robotic engineer, gaseous exchange is foundational to: - **Mechanical ventilator design** (pressure-cycled vs. volume-cycled). - **Pulse oximetry** and **capnography** algorithms. - **Anesthesia machines** and closed-circuit breathing systems. - **CPAP/BiPAP** devices for sleep apnea. - **Artificial lungs (ECMO)** membrane oxygenator design. - **High-altitude / hyperbaric** physiology modeling. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Physiological Constants to Memorize:** > - Atmospheric pressure at sea level: **$P_{atm} = 760$ mmHg** (101.325 kPa) > - Partial pressure of $O_2$ in atmospheric air: **$P_{IO_2} = 160$ mmHg** > - Partial pressure of $O_2$ in alveoli: **$P_{AO_2} = 104$ mmHg** > - Partial pressure of $O_2$ in arterial blood: **$P_{aO_2} = 95$ mmHg** > - Partial pressure of $O_2$ in tissue cells: **$P_{tO_2} = 40$ mmHg** > - Partial pressure of $CO_2$ in alveoli: **$P_{ACO_2} = 40$ mmHg** > - Partial pressure of $CO_2$ in venous blood: **$P_{vCO_2} = 46$ mmHg** > - Surface area of respiratory membrane: **≈ 70 m²** > - Thickness of respiratory membrane: **≈ 0.6 µm** --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Respiratory Membrane — Anatomy for Engineers The respiratory (alveolar–capillary) membrane consists of **six layers**: 1. Surfactant layer (monolayer of phospholipids + water). 2. Type I alveolar epithelial cell (squamous). 3. Fused basal laminae of epithelium and capillary endothelium. 4. Capillary endothelial cell. 5. Plasma in the capillary. 6. Red blood cell (RBC) membrane. The membrane is **~0.6 µm thick** and presents a **surface area of ~70 m²** (≈ the size of a tennis court), which is the *engineering figure of merit* for diffusion capacity. ## 2.2 Mechanics of Breathing — Why Air Moves Air flows into the lungs because of a **pressure gradient** between the atmosphere and the intrapulmonary (alveolar) pressure. This gradient is generated by changing the **volume** of the thoracic cavity. > [!NOTE] > **Two Physics Laws Govern Ventilation:** > - **Boyle's Law** (generates airflow): $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ at constant temperature. > - **Fick's Law of Diffusion** (governs gas transfer across membrane). ### 2.2.1 Inspiration (Active Process) - **Diaphragm** contracts and flattens (moves ~1 cm at rest, up to 10 cm in deep breathing). - **External intercostal muscles** contract, lifting the rib cage. - **Thoracic volume ↑** → **Intrapleural pressure ↓** (becomes more sub-atmospheric, from ~–4 mmHg to ~–6 mmHg). - **Alveolar pressure ↓** to ~ **–1 mmHg** (sub-atmospheric). - Since $P_{atm}$ (760 mmHg) > $P_{alveolar}$ (759 mmHg), air flows **inward**. ### 2.2.2 Expiration (Passive at Rest) - Diaphragm and external intercostals **relax**. - Thoracic volume ↓ → Intrapleural pressure returns to ~ **–4 mmHg**. - Alveolar pressure rises to ~ **+1 mmHg**. - Since $P_{alveolar}$ > $P_{atm}$, air flows **outward**. - **Forced expiration** additionally recruits **internal intercostals** and **abdominal muscles**. ## 2.3 The Gas Laws — Engineer's Toolkit | # | Law | Equation | Physiological Use | |---|-----|----------|-------------------| | 1 | Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures | $P_{total} = \sum P_i$ | Calculating partial pressure of $O_2$ in air | | 2 | Boyle's Law | $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ | Mechanics of breathing | | 3 | Henry's Law | $C = k_H \cdot P$ | Dissolving gases in blood plasma | | 4 | Fick's Law of Diffusion | $V_{gas} = \frac{A \cdot D \cdot (P_1 - P_2)}{T}$ | Gas transfer across respiratory membrane | | 5 | Graham's Law | $\text{Rate} \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{MW}}$ | $CO_2$ diffuses faster than $O_2$ | | 6 | Ideal Gas Law | $PV = nRT$ | Lung compliance and gas volumes | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fick's Law** is the *master equation* for gaseous exchange in the alveoli. Note that the **rate of gas transfer is inversely proportional to membrane thickness ($T$)** and **directly proportional to surface area ($A$)** and partial pressure difference. This is why diseases like **pulmonary fibrosis** (thickened membrane → slow exchange) and **emphysema** (reduced surface area → slow exchange) cripple gas exchange. ## 2.4 Lung Volumes and Capacities (Spirogram Data) A **capacity** is the sum of two or more lung **volumes**. | Symbol | Parameter | Typical Value (Adult) | |--------|-----------|----------------------| | $TV$ | Tidal Volume | **500 mL** | | $IRV$ | Inspiratory Reserve Volume | **3000 mL** | | $ERV$ | Expiratory Reserve Volume | **1100 mL** | | $RV$ | Residual Volume | **1200 mL** | | $VC$ | Vital Capacity $= TV + IRV + ERV$ | **4600 mL** | | $IC$ | Inspiratory Capacity $= TV + IRV$ | **3500 mL** | | $FRC$ | Functional Residual Capacity $= ERV + RV$ | **2300 mL** | | $TLC$ | Total Lung Capacity $= VC + RV$ | **5800 mL** | > [!NOTE] > **Residual Volume (RV)** cannot be measured by spirometry alone — it requires **helium dilution**, **nitrogen washout**, or **body plethysmography**. This is a common KTU question. ## 2.5 The Alveolar Gas Equation The alveolar oxygen partial pressure ($P_{AO_2}$) is computed as: $$P_{AO_2} = F_{IO_2} \cdot (P_{atm} - P_{H_2O}) - \frac{P_{aCO_2}}{R}$$ Where: - $F_{IO_2}$ = Fractional concentration of inspired $O_2$ (0.21 in room air) - $P_{H_2O}$ = Water vapor pressure at body temp = **47 mmHg** - $P_{aCO_2}$ = Arterial $CO_2$ partial pressure - $R$ = Respiratory quotient = 0.8 (at rest) > [!TIP] > This is the **single most important equation** in respiratory physiology. Engineers designing ventilators set $F_{IO_2}$ to control $P_{AO_2}$ and thus oxygen delivery. ## 2.6 Oxygen Transport — Two Parallel Mechanisms 1. **Dissolved in plasma** (~3%): obeys Henry's Law, $C_{O_2} = k_H \cdot P_{O_2}$. 2. **Bound to Hemoglobin (Hb)** (~97%): forms **oxyhemoglobin**, described by the **Oxygen–Hemoglobin Dissociation Curve** (sigmoidal). **Total $O_2$ content equation:** $$C_{O_2} = (1.34 \cdot Hb \cdot S_{O_2}) + (0.003 \cdot P_{O_2})$$ Where: - $1.34$ = mL $O_2$ bound per gram Hb (Hüfner constant) - $Hb$ = Hemoglobin concentration (g/dL) - $S_{O_2}$ = % saturation of Hb - $0.003$ = solubility coefficient of $O_2$ in plasma (mL $O_2$ / 100 mL blood / mmHg) ## 2.7 Carbon Dioxide Transport — Three Forms | Form | % Carried | Notes | |------|-----------|-------| | Dissolved in plasma | **7%** | $CO_2$ is ~20× more soluble than $O_2$ | | Carbamino compounds (bound to Hb) | **23%** | Binds to globin chain, not heme | | Bicarbonate ions ($HCO_3^{-}$) | **70%** | Dominant mechanism, via carbonic anhydrase | **Key reaction (RBC interior):** $$CO_2 + H_2O \xrightleftharpoons[\text{carbonic anhydrase}]{} H_2CO_3 \xrightleftharpoons{} H^+ + HCO_3^-$$ The **chloride shift** (Hamburger shift) moves $HCO_3^-$ out of the RBC in exchange for $Cl^-$. ## 2.8 The Oxygen–Hemoglobin Dissociation Curve The **S-shaped (sigmoidal) curve** shows % saturation of Hb vs. $P_{O_2}$: - $P_{O_2} = 60$ mmHg → ~90% saturation (loading plateau). - $P_{O_2} = 40$ mmHg (tissue) → ~75% saturation (unloads ~25% of $O_2$). - $P_{50} \approx 26$ mmHg (50% saturation). ### 2.8.1 The Bohr Effect > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bohr Effect:** Increased $P_{CO_2}$, decreased pH (more $H^+$), increased temperature, and increased **2,3-BPG** all **shift the curve to the right**, **decreasing Hb's affinity for $O_2$** — *favoring $O_2$ release* in metabolically active tissues. > Conversely, the **left shift** (decreased $P_{CO_2}$, increased pH, decreased temperature, decreased 2,3-BPG, fetal Hb) **increases** $O_2$ affinity. ### 2.8.2 The Haldane Effect Oxygenation of blood in the lungs displaces $CO_2$ from Hb, facilitating $CO_2$ release. ## 2.9 Neural & Chemical Control of Respiration - **Medullary respiratory center**: Dorsal Respiratory Group (DRG, sets rhythm) and Ventral Respiratory Group (VRG, forced breathing). - **Pontine respiratory group**: Pneumotaxic center (limits inspiration) and apneustic center (promotes inspiration). - **Chemoreceptors**: - **Central** (medulla): sensitive to ↑ $P_{CO_2}$ / ↓ pH of CSF. - **Peripheral** (carotid and aortic bodies): sensitive to ↓ $P_{O_2}$, ↑ $P_{CO_2}$, ↓ pH. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Partial Pressure of a Gas in a Mixture (Dalton's Law) **Statement:** The total pressure exerted by a mixture of non-reacting gases is the sum of the partial pressures of the individual gases. **Derivation:** Consider atmospheric air. By volume (and therefore by mole fraction, since gases are ideal), air is: - $N_2$ : 78.62% - $O_2$ : 20.84% - $Ar$ : 0.93% - $CO_2$ : 0.04% - Other trace gases: balance For a gas $i$ in a mixture: $$P_i = X_i \cdot P_{total}$$ Where $X_i$ is the mole fraction. For oxygen in dry air at sea level: $$P_{IO_2} = 0.2094 \cdot 760 = 158.98 \approx 160 \text{ mmHg}$$ > When the gas is *humidified* (as it is when entering the respiratory tract), water vapor pressure must be subtracted: $$P_{IO_2} = F_{IO_2} \cdot (P_{atm} - P_{H_2O})$$ $$P_{IO_2} = 0.2094 \cdot (760 - 47) = 0.2094 \cdot 713 = 149.3 \text{ mmHg}$$ This is the **tracheal** $P_{O_2}$. It drops further to ~104 mmHg in the alveoli because of the constant addition of $CO_2$ from the blood. ## 3.2 Derivation: Alveolar Gas Equation **Goal:** Find an expression for $P_{AO_2}$ in terms of measurable quantities. **Step 1 — Start with conservation of mass (oxygen):** Oxygen entering alveoli = Oxygen leaving alveoli + Oxygen consumed. Algebraically, the alveolar ventilation equation gives: $$P_{AO_2} = P_{IO_2} - \frac{\dot{V}_{O_2}}{\dot{V}_A} \cdot K$$ where $\dot{V}_{O_2}$ is oxygen consumption, $\dot{V}_A$ is alveolar ventilation, and $K$ is a constant. **Step 2 — Introduce Respiratory Quotient:** $$R = \frac{\dot{V}_{CO_2}}{\dot{V}_{O_2}} \approx 0.8 \text{ (rest, mixed diet)}$$ So $\dot{V}_{O_2} = \dot{V}_{CO_2} / R$. **Step 3 — Substitute $P_{aCO_2}$ (a clinical surrogate for $\dot{V}_{CO_2}/\dot{V}_A$):** In steady state, alveolar $P_{CO_2} =$ arterial $P_{CO_2}$, and: $$P_{aCO_2} = \frac{\dot{V}_{CO_2} \cdot K}{\dot{V}_A} \Rightarrow \frac{\dot{V}_{O_2}}{\dot{V}_A} \cdot K = \frac{P_{aCO_2}}{R}$$ **Step 4 — Final equation:** $$P_{AO_2} = F_{IO_2} \cdot (P_{atm} - P_{H_2O}) - \frac{P_{aCO_2}}{R}$$ **Numerical Verification (room air, normal subject):** $$P_{AO_2} = 0.21 \cdot (760 - 47) - \frac{40}{0.8}$$ $$P_{AO_2} = 0.21 \cdot 713 - 50$$ $$P_{AO_2} = 149.73 - 50 = 99.73 \text{ mmHg} \approx 100 \text{ mmHg}$$ (Some texts quote 104 mmHg by including a small **correction factor** $(1-R) \cdot F_{IO_2}$, but the simplified form above is sufficient for KTU.) ## 3.3 Worked Example 1: Total Oxygen Content of Arterial Blood **Given:** $Hb = 15$ g/dL, $S_{O_2} = 97\%$, $P_{O_2} = 95$ mmHg. **Step 1 — Dissolved $O_2$:** $$C_{dissolved} = 0.003 \cdot 95 = 0.285 \text{ mL/dL}$$ **Step 2 — Hb-bound $O_2$:** $$C_{bound} = 1.34 \cdot 15 \cdot 0.97 = 19.50 \text{ mL/dL}$$ **Step 3 — Total:** $$C_{O_2} = 19.50 + 0.285 = 19.79 \text{ mL/dL}$$ **Interpretation:** ~98.5% of arterial $O_2$ is Hb-bound; only ~1.5% is dissolved. This is why **hemoglobin disorders** (anemia, sickle cell) devastate oxygen delivery. ## 3.4 Worked Example 2: Fick's Law of Diffusion — Effect of Emphysema In **emphysema**, alveolar walls break down, reducing surface area from 70 m² to 30 m². Membrane thickness is unchanged. By how much is diffusion reduced? $$V_{gas} \propto A \text{ (with } D, \Delta P, T \text{ constant)}$$ $$\frac{V_{emphysema}}{V_{normal}} = \frac{30}{70} = 0.429$$ **Result:** Diffusion capacity falls to **~43%** of normal — gas exchange is severely impaired. This is exactly why emphysema patients require supplemental $O_2$ and have dyspnea on exertion. ## 3.5 Worked Example 3: Effect of Altitude on Alveolar $P_{O_2}$ At the summit of Mt. Everest (~8848 m), atmospheric pressure ≈ 250 mmHg. Compute $P_{AO_2}$ assuming $P_{aCO_2} = 25$ mmHg (hyperventilated) and $R = 0.8$. **Step 1:** $$P_{IO_2} = 0.21 \cdot (250 - 47) = 0.21 \cdot 203 = 42.63 \text{ mmHg}$$ **Step 2:** $$P_{AO_2} = 42.63 - \frac{25}{0.8} = 42.63 - 31.25 = 11.38 \text{ mmHg}$$ **Interpretation:** This is barely above the $P_{50}$ of Hb (26 mmHg) — at this $P_{AO_2}$, Hb is only ~50–60% saturated. Sustained human life at this altitude is at the absolute limit, explaining the famous "death zone" (>8000 m). ## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Pathophysiology of Gaseous Exchange | Condition | Surface Area (A) | Thickness (T) | $\Delta P$ | Clinical Effect | |-----------|-----------------|---------------|-----------|-----------------| | Healthy adult | 70 m² | 0.6 µm | Normal | Normal gas exchange | | Emphysema | ↓↓ to 30 m² | Normal | Normal | Hypoxemia, dyspnea | | Pulmonary fibrosis | Normal | ↑↑ to 5 µm | Normal | Hypoxemia, dry cough | | Pulmonary edema | Normal | ↑ (fluid) | Normal | Severe hypoxemia | | High altitude | Normal | Normal | ↓ (low $P_{IO_2}$) | Hypoxia, hyperventilation | | Asthma (acute) | Normal | Slight ↑ | Normal | Wheezing, reversible hypoxia | | Pneumonia | ↓ (consolidation) | ↑ | Normal | Hypoxemia + fever | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: The Gaseous Exchange Pipeline The diagram below maps the **flow of air and blood** through the respiratory system, the engineering equivalent of a *gas–liquid mass-transfer contactor*. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Atmospheric Air
PO2 = 160 mmHg] --> B[Nose and Nasal Cavity
Filtration Humidification Warming] B --> C[Pharynx and Larynx] C --> D[Trachea] D --> E[Primary Bronchi Left and Right] E --> F[Bronchioles
16 generations] F --> G[Respiratory Bronchioles
Alveolar Ducts] G --> H[Alveolar Sacs
300 million alveoli] H --> I[Respiratory Membrane
Thickness 0.6 micrometre] I --> J[Pulmonary Capillary Blood
PO2 rises 40 to 95 mmHg] J --> K[Pulmonary Vein] K --> L[Left Atrium and Left Ventricle] L --> M[Systemic Arterial Blood] M --> N[Systemic Capillaries
PO2 falls 95 to 40 mmHg] N --> O[Tissue Cells
PO2 approx 40 mmHg] O --> P[Systemic Venous Blood
PO2 = 40 mmHg] P --> Q[Right Atrium and Right Ventricle] Q --> R[Pulmonary Artery] R --> J ``` ## 4.2 Alveolar–Capillary Gas Exchange — Block Detail ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ALV["ALVEOLUS"] A1[Inspired Air
PAO2 = 104 mmHg] A2[Alveolar PCO2
PACO2 = 40 mmHg] end subgraph MEM["RESPIRATORY MEMBRANE 0.6 micrometre"] M1[Surfactant Layer] M2[Type I Pneumocyte] M3[Fused Basal Lamina] M4[Capillary Endothelium] end subgraph CAP["PULMONARY CAPILLARY"] C1[Incoming Venous Blood
PvO2 = 40 mmHg] C2[RBC with Deoxyhemoglobin] C3[Outgoing Arterial Blood
PaO2 = 95 mmHg] C4[RBC with Oxyhemoglobin 97 percent] end A1 -- Diffusion across MEM --> C1 C1 -- O2 binds Hb --> C2 C2 -- Becomes --> C4 C4 -- Exits capillary --> C3 A2 -- CO2 diffuses out --> C1 ``` ## 4.3 Control of Respiration — Feedback Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CNS["CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM"] DRG[Dorsal Respiratory Group
Basic Rhythm] VRG[Ventral Respiratory Group
Forced Breathing] PNC[Pontine Centers
Pneumotaxic and Apneustic] end subgraph CHEMO["CHEMORECEPTORS"] CC[Central Chemoreceptors
Medulla pH and PCO2] PC[Peripheral Chemoreceptors
Carotid and Aortic Bodies
PO2 PCO2 pH] end subgraph EFF["EFFECTOR ORGANS"] DIAPH[Diaphragm] ICM[Intercostal Muscles] AC[Abdominal Muscles] end DRG --> DIAPH VRG --> ICM PNC --> DRG CC --> DRG PC --> DRG CC --> VRG PC --> VRG DIAPH --> LUNGS[Lungs] ICM --> LUNGS LUNGS -- Blood Gases --> CHEMO LUNGS -- Mechano Signals --> PNC ``` ## 4.4 Oxygen–Hemoglobin Dissociation — State Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LUNGS["LUNGS: HIGH PO2 LOW PCO2"] L1[PaO2 = 95 mmHg] L2[PCO2 = 40 mmHg] L3[pH = 7.40] L4[Temperature = 37 C] end subgraph TISSUE["TISSUES: LOW PO2 HIGH PCO2"] T1[PtO2 = 40 mmHg] T2[PtCO2 = 46 mmHg] T3[pH = 7.36] T4[Temperature = 38 C] end LUNGS -- Bohr Effect LEFT shift
O2 loads onto Hb --> HEMOGLOBIN[Oxyhemoglobin
Saturation 97 percent] HEMOGLOBIN -- Travels via arterial blood --> TISSUE TISSUE -- Bohr Effect RIGHT shift
O2 unloads from Hb --> DEOXY[Deoxyhemoglobin
Saturation 75 percent] DEOXY -- Returns via venous blood --> LUNGS ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define gaseous exchange. List the four phases of respiration with one-line descriptions.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Gaseous exchange is the biophysical process by which oxygen diffuses from alveolar air into pulmonary capillary blood and carbon dioxide diffuses in the reverse direction, driven by partial pressure gradients across the respiratory membrane (thickness ≈ **0.6 µm**). The four phases are: 1. **Pulmonary Ventilation** – Bulk movement of air into/out of lungs. 2. **External Respiration** – Diffusion of $O_2$ and $CO_2$ between alveoli and pulmonary blood. 3. **Transport of Gases** – Movement of $O_2$ and $CO_2$ in blood between lungs and tissues. 4. **Internal Respiration** – Diffusion of gases between systemic capillaries and tissue cells. *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four phases.)* --- ### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **What is the Bohr Effect? Mention two physiological conditions that cause a rightward shift of the oxygen–hemoglobin dissociation curve.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Bohr Effect** describes the phenomenon in which an increase in $P_{CO_2}$ and/or $H^+$ concentration (decrease in pH), and/or an increase in temperature, **decreases hemoglobin's affinity for oxygen**, shifting the ODC to the right and favoring $O_2$ release at the tissues. (2 marks) Two physiological conditions causing rightward shift: - **Increased metabolic activity** (e.g., exercising muscle): produces more $CO_2$ and $H^+$, raising local $P_{CO_2}$ and lowering pH. - **Increased body temperature** (fever or exercise): reduces Hb affinity for $O_2$. *(1 mark for the two conditions.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Gaseous Exchange:** > 1. **Confusing "Pulmonary Ventilation" with "Gaseous Exchange"** — many students describe Boyle's law (ventilation) when asked about diffusion (exchange). The KTU paper specifically asks about *exchange*, so Fick's law, partial pressure gradients, and the respiratory membrane are the expected focus. > 2. **Mixing up $P_{AO_2}$ (104) and $P_{IO_2}$ (160)** — remember 160 is for dry atmospheric air, 104 is alveolar. > 3. **Forgetting to subtract $P_{H_2O}$** in the alveolar gas equation (use $P_{atm} - 47$). > 4. **Saying $O_2$ is "transported mostly in dissolved form"** — this is wrong; ~97% is Hb-bound. > 5. **Drawing the oxygen–hemoglobin curve as hyperbolic** — it is **sigmoidal** (S-shaped) due to cooperative binding. > 6. **Skipping the chloride (Hamburger) shift** in $CO_2$ transport — KTU specifically tests this. ### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **(a) [7 marks]** Describe in detail the **mechanics of inspiration and expiration** during quiet breathing. Include the role of the diaphragm, intrapleural pressure changes, and apply **Boyle's law** to explain airflow. **(b) [7 marks]** State and explain **Fick's law of diffusion** as applied to gaseous exchange across the respiratory membrane. Discuss how this law explains the pathophysiology of (i) pulmonary fibrosis and (ii) emphysema. #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 marks:** **Inspiration (active, 4 marks):** - Quiet inspiration is initiated by the **medullary inspiratory center** sending signals via the phrenic nerve. - The **diaphragm** contracts, flattening inferiorly and increasing the vertical diameter of the thoracic cavity by ~1 cm (up to 10 cm in deep inspiration). - The **external intercostal muscles** contract, lifting the ribs upward and outward, increasing the anteroposterior and lateral diameters. - This increases **thoracic volume** ($V_2 > V_1$). - By Boyle's Law ($P_1V_1 = P_2V_2$), as volume increases, **intrapulmonary (alveolar) pressure drops** from 760 mmHg to about **759 mmHg** (i.e., –1 mmHg relative to atmosphere). - The **intrathoracic (intrapleural) pressure** becomes more sub-atmospheric (~–6 mmHg from a baseline of –4 mmHg), due to the opposing recoil of the lungs. - Since atmospheric pressure > alveolar pressure, **air flows inward** down its pressure gradient. **Expiration (passive at rest, 3 marks):** - The inspiratory muscles **relax** and the elastic recoil of lung tissue and chest wall passively compresses the lungs. - Thoracic volume decreases → alveolar pressure rises to **+1 mmHg** above atmospheric. - Intrapleural pressure returns to baseline of **–4 mmHg**. - Since alveolar pressure > atmospheric pressure, **air flows outward**. - During **forced expiration**, the internal intercostals and abdominal muscles (rectus abdominis) contract to further reduce thoracic volume. --- **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Fick's Law (4 marks):** The rate of gas transfer across a membrane is given by: $$V_{gas} = \frac{A \cdot D \cdot (P_1 - P_2)}{T}$$ Where: - $V_{gas}$ = volume of gas transferred per unit time (mL/min) - $A$ = surface area of the membrane (m²) - $D$ = diffusion coefficient of the gas (depends on solubility and $\sqrt{1/MW}$) - $P_1 - P_2$ = partial pressure difference across the membrane (mmHg) - $T$ = thickness of the membrane (µm) For the respiratory membrane: $A \approx 70$ m², $T \approx 0.6$ µm, and the pressure difference is maintained by continuous ventilation (lungs) and continuous blood flow (capillaries). **Clinical applications (3 marks):** (i) **Pulmonary fibrosis:** The alveolar–capillary membrane becomes thickened due to deposition of collagen and fibrous tissue (T rises from 0.6 µm to ~5 µm). Since $V_{gas} \propto 1/T$, diffusion rate drops to ~12% of normal. Patients develop **hypoxemia**, dry cough, and "Velcro" crackles on auscultation. The condition typically requires supplemental oxygen and anti-fibrotic therapy. (ii) **Emphysema:** Destruction of alveolar walls reduces the **surface area** $A$ from 70 m² to 30 m² or less. Since $V_{gas} \propto A$, diffusion rate falls to ~43% of normal. Patients exhibit barrel chest, pursed-lip breathing, and dyspnea on minimal exertion. Oxygen therapy is mainstay treatment; no curative therapy exists for advanced disease. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **transport of carbon dioxide** in blood. Describe the three forms with their percentages, the role of **carbonic anhydrase**, and the **chloride (Hamburger) shift**. **(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **neural regulation of respiration**. Include the roles of the dorsal and ventral respiratory groups, pneumotaxic and apneustic centers, and the role of central and peripheral chemoreceptors. #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 7 marks:** $CO_2$ is transported from tissues to the lungs in three forms: 1. **Dissolved in plasma (~7%)**: $CO_2$ is ~20 times more soluble than $O_2$, so a meaningful fraction dissolves directly. This is the form measured by **capnography** in ICUs. (1 mark) 2. **Carbamino compounds (~23%)**: $CO_2$ binds reversibly to the **amino ($NH_2$) groups of globin chains** in the Hb molecule, forming *carbaminohemoglobin*. This occurs at sites distinct from the heme iron where $O_2$ binds. (1 mark) 3. **Bicarbonate ions (~70%)** — the dominant mechanism: (3 marks) Inside the **RBC**, the enzyme **carbonic anhydrase** catalyzes: $$CO_2 + H_2O \xrightleftharpoons[\text{carbonic anhydrase}]{} H_2CO_3 \xrightleftharpoons{} H^+ + HCO_3^-$$ The $HCO_3^-$ produced diffuses out of the RBC into plasma. To maintain electrochemical neutrality, **chloride ions ($Cl^-$) move from plasma into the RBC** — this is the **chloride (Hamburger) shift**, mediated by the **Band 3 anion exchanger (AE1)**. The $H^+$ ions are buffered by binding to **deoxyhemoglobin** (which is a better buffer than oxyhemoglobin — the *Haldane effect*). This is why deoxygenated blood can carry more $CO_2$ than oxygenated blood. In the **pulmonary capillaries**, the entire process reverses: $CO_2$ is exhaled, $Cl^-$ exits the RBC, and $Hb$ picks up $O_2$. **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Medullary centers (3 marks):** - The **Dorsal Respiratory Group (DRG)** in the medulla generates the **basic rhythmic cycle** of breathing (~12–15 breaths/min at rest). It is responsible for **inspiration**. - The **Ventral Respiratory Group (VRG)** is inactive during quiet breathing but becomes active during **forced breathing**, recruiting accessory muscles for forced inspiration and expiration. **Pontine centers (2 marks):** - The **Pneumotaxic center** in the upper pons *limits* inspiration by sending inhibitory signals to the DRG, allowing for smooth transition to expiration. It increases respiratory rate. - The **Apneustic center** in the lower pons *promotes* and *prolongs* inspiration, increasing tidal volume. The two pontine centers work in opposition to fine-tune the breathing pattern. **Chemoreceptors (2 marks):** - **Central chemoreceptors** (ventrolateral medulla): sense ↑ $P_{CO_2}$ / ↓ pH of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF). $CO_2$ diffuses into CSF, forms $H^+$ via carbonic anhydrase, and the resulting acidosis stimulates increased ventilation. *These are the dominant controllers of ventilation at rest.* - **Peripheral chemoreceptors** (carotid bodies at the bifurcation of common carotid arteries; aortic bodies in the aortic arch): sensitive to ↓ $P_{O_2}$ (especially below 60 mmHg), ↑ $P_{CO_2}$, and ↓ pH. They are the *only* receptors that respond directly to hypoxemia and are vital at high altitude. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for Gaseous Exchange:** ### Definitions - ✅ Gaseous exchange = diffusion of $O_2$ and $CO_2$ across the respiratory membrane by partial pressure gradient. - ✅ Respiratory membrane = 6 layers, thickness **0.6 µm**, surface area **70 m²**. - ✅ Tidal volume = **500 mL**; Vital capacity = **4600 mL**; TLC ≈ **5800 mL**. ### Laws - ✅ **Dalton's Law**: $P_i = X_i \cdot P_{total}$ → $P_{IO_2} = 0.21 \cdot 713 = 150$ mmHg in humidified tracheal air. - ✅ **Boyle's Law**: $P_1V_1 = P_2V_2$ → drives ventilation. - ✅ **Henry's Law**: $C = k_H \cdot P$ → dissolved gas in plasma. - ✅ **Fick's Law**: $V_{gas} = \frac{A \cdot D \cdot \Delta P}{T}$ → alveolar gas transfer. - ✅ **Alveolar Gas Equation**: $P_{AO_2} = F_{IO_2} \cdot (P_{atm} - 47) - \frac{P_{aCO_2}}{R}$. ### Pressure Values (Memorize) - ✅ Atmospheric: 760 mmHg; Alveolar $O_2$: **104 mmHg**; Alveolar $CO_2$: **40 mmHg**; Tissue $O_2$: **40 mmHg**; Arterial $O_2$: **95 mmHg**. ### Oxygen Transport - ✅ 97% bound to Hb (sigmoidal curve, $P_{50} = 26$ mmHg); 3% dissolved. - ✅ Total $O_2$ content = $(1.34 \cdot Hb \cdot S_{O_2}) + (0.003 \cdot P_{O_2})$. ### Carbon Dioxide Transport - ✅ 70% as $HCO_3^-$ (via carbonic anhydrase); 23% as carbamino-Hb; 7% dissolved. - ✅ **Chloride (Hamburger) shift** maintains electrochemical balance. - ✅ **Haldane Effect**: oxygenation of Hb promotes $CO_2$ release. ### Bohr Effect (Right Shift = ↓ Hb Affinity) - ✅ Triggers: ↑ $P_{CO_2}$, ↑ $H^+$, ↑ temperature, ↑ 2,3-BPG. - ✅ Physiological purpose: deliver more $O_2$ to actively metabolizing tissues. ### Control of Respiration - ✅ DRG = basic rhythm; VRG = forced breathing. - ✅ Pneumotaxic = limits inspiration (↑ rate); Apneustic = prolongs inspiration (↓ rate). - ✅ Central chemoreceptors respond to ↑ $P_{CO_2}$ (via CSF pH). - ✅ Peripheral chemoreceptors respond to ↓ $P_{O_2}$ (below 60 mmHg), ↑ $P_{CO_2}$, ↓ pH. ### Engineering & Clinical Correlates - ✅ Pulse oximeter measures $S_{pO_2}$ (functional Hb saturation). - ✅ Capnograph measures end-tidal $P_{CO_2}$ (surrogate for $P_{aCO_2}$). - ✅ Ventilator settings: control $F_{IO_2}$, tidal volume, respiratory rate, PEEP. - ✅ ECMO provides external gaseous exchange via a membrane oxygenator. ---

topics.title: Gas Transport
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBRT205 - ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY FOR BIOMEDICAL ENGINEERING (BIOMEDICAL AND ROBOTIC ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gas Transport

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 3 — Gas Transport

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology)**
> **Gas Transport** refers to the physiological movement of respiratory gases — primarily **Oxygen (O₂)** and **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** — between the external environment (alveoli of the lungs), the bloodstream, and the intracellular mitochondria where cellular respiration occurs. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this involves the biophysical mechanisms of **convective bulk flow**, **passive diffusion** governed by **Fick's Law**, and the **reversible chemical binding** of gases to specialized transport proteins (chiefly **Hemoglobin**, abbreviated **Hb**).

---

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the human body as a vast industrial city. The **lungs** are the *power plant's intake*, the **bloodstream** is the *highway network*, the **red blood cells (RBCs/Erythrocytes)** are the *specialized cargo trucks*, and the **mitochondria** are the *factories* that consume the fuel.

- **Oxygen (O₂)** is the *raw fuel* that must be picked up at the power plant (alveoli) and delivered to every factory (cell).
- **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** is the *waste gas* that must be collected from every factory and returned to the plant's exhaust vent (alveoli → exhaled air).

Just as a truck cannot carry loose fuel, most O₂ is **not** dissolved freely in the watery plasma. Instead, it is **loaded onto a carrier molecule — Hemoglobin (Hb)** — which acts like a magnetic cargo clamp. Each Hb molecule can carry up to **4 oxygen molecules**, forming **Oxyhemoglobin (HbO₂)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why can't plasma alone carry enough oxygen?**
> Plasma is mostly water. The solubility of O₂ in water at body temperature (37°C) is extremely low — only about **3 mL O₂ per 100 mL of plasma** at alveolar partial pressure. This is *insufficient* for metabolic demand (which is ~250 mL O₂/min at rest). Hemoglobin solves this by increasing the blood's O₂ carrying capacity by a factor of approximately **70×**.

---

### 1.2 The Three Modes of Respiratory Gas Movement

| Mode | Driving Force | Where It Operates | Governing Principle |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Convection (Bulk Flow)** | Pressure gradient created by the heart pump | Systemic & pulmonary circulation | Cardiac output physiology |
| **Diffusion (Passive)** | Partial pressure gradient | Alveolus ↔ Capillary ↔ Tissue | **Fick's Law of Diffusion** |
| **Chemical Binding** | Reversible affinity (saturation kinetics) | Within RBCs (to Hemoglobin) | **Oxygen-Hemoglobin Dissociation Curve** |

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (KTU 2024 Key Values to Memorize)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Constants for KTU University Examination**
> - **Alveolar Partial Pressure of O₂ (PₐO₂):** **104 mmHg**
> - **Alveolar Partial Pressure of CO₂ (PₐCO₂):** **40 mmHg**
> - **Venous Partial Pressure of O₂ (PvO₂):** **40 mmHg**
> - **Venous Partial Pressure of CO₂ (PvCO₂):** **46 mmHg**
> - **Arterial Partial Pressure of O₂ (PaO₂):** **95–100 mmHg**
> - **Arterial Partial Pressure of CO₂ (PaCO₂):** **40 mmHg**
> - **Tissue Partial Pressure of O₂:** **~20 mmHg** (mitochondrial demand zone)
> - **Body Temperature:** **310 K (37°C)**
> - **Oxygen Carrying Capacity of Hb:** **1.34 mL O₂ per gram of Hb**
> - **Normal Hemoglobin Concentration:** **15 g/dL** (men: 14–18, women: 12–16)
> - **Total Blood O₂ Content (CaO₂):** ~**20 mL O₂/dL blood**
> - **Bunsen Solubility Coefficient of O₂ in plasma:** **0.0031 mL O₂/(100 mL blood·mmHg)**
> - **Bunsen Solubility Coefficient of CO₂ in plasma:** **0.067 mL CO₂/(100 mL blood·mmHg)**

---

### 1.4 Henry's Law — The Foundation of Dissolved Gas Transport

When a gas comes into contact with a liquid, the amount that **dissolves** in the liquid is directly proportional to the **partial pressure** of that gas above the liquid. This is **Henry's Law**.

$$C_{dissolved} = \alpha \cdot P_{gas}$$

Where:
- $C_{dissolved}$ = concentration of gas dissolved in the liquid (mL gas / 100 mL blood)
- $\alpha$ = **Bunsen Solubility Coefficient** (mL gas / 100 mL blood / mmHg)
- $P_{gas}$ = Partial pressure of the gas (mmHg)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy for Henry's Law:**
> Think of a fizzy soft drink bottle. Before opening, CO₂ is dissolved under high pressure (high $P_{CO_2}$). When you open the cap, the pressure above the liquid drops, and bubbles escape until a new equilibrium is reached. The amount of CO₂ that *stays* dissolved depends on the pressure above it. In the body, alveolar $P_{O_2}$ and $P_{CO_2}$ act exactly like that "pressure cap."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Oxygen Transport — The Two Parallel Pathways

Oxygen is carried in blood via **two simultaneous mechanisms**:

#### Pathway 1: Dissolved in Plasma (Henry's Law Component)

$$C_{O_2,\,dissolved} = \alpha_{O_2} \cdot P_{O_2}$$

Using $\alpha_{O_2} = 0.0031$ mL/dL/mmHg and arterial $P_{O_2} = 100$ mmHg:

$$C_{O_2,\,dissolved} = 0.0031 \times 100 = 0.31 \text{ mL O}_2/\text{dL blood}$$

This is **negligibly small** — only ~1.5% of total O₂ content.

#### Pathway 2: Bound to Hemoglobin (The Dominant Mechanism)

$$C_{O_2,\,Hb} = [Hb] \times 1.34 \times S_{O_2}$$

Where:
- $[Hb]$ = Hemoglobin concentration (g/dL)
- $1.34$ = Hüfner's constant (mL O₂ bound per gram Hb at 100% saturation)
- $S_{O_2}$ = Fractional O₂ saturation of Hb (0 to 1)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 1.34 and not 1.39?**
> Historically, the theoretical value is **1.39 mL O₂/g Hb** (based on Hb molecular weight 64,458 Da × 4 binding sites). However, in vivo, some Hb is present as methemoglobin and carboxyhemoglobin, which cannot bind O₂. The **empirically measured** value is **1.34 mL O₂/g Hb**, which is the value KTU examiners expect for calculations.

---

### 2.2 Total Oxygen Content Equation (Foley's Equation)

The total oxygen content in arterial blood is the **sum** of dissolved and Hb-bound oxygen:

$$C_{aO_2} = (\alpha_{O_2} \times P_{aO_2}) + ([Hb] \times 1.34 \times S_{aO_2})$$

For a healthy adult: $C_{aO_2} \approx 0.31 + 15 \times 1.34 \times 0.97 \approx 19.8$ **mL O₂/dL blood**.

---

### 2.3 The Oxygen-Hemoglobin Dissociation Curve (OHDC)

The OHDC is a **sigmoidal (S-shaped) plot** of:
- **X-axis:** $P_{O_2}$ (mmHg), ranging 0–100
- **Y-axis:** $S_{O_2}$ (%), ranging 0–100

**Key Reference Points (Memorize for KTU):**

| Point | $P_{O_2}$ (mmHg) | $S_{O_2}$ (%) | Physiological Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mixed Venous Blood | 40 | **75** | Returning from tissues |
| Normal Arterial | 100 | **97–98** | Leaving alveoli |
| 50% Saturation (P₅₀) | **26–27** | **50** | Curve standard reference |
| Tissue Capillary (exit) | 20 | ~30–35 | At mitochondrial O₂ demand zone |
| Max Loading Plateau | 60 | ~89–90 | Alveolar-capillary interface |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sigmoidal Shape — Why S-shaped, not a straight line?**
> Hemoglobin is a **tetramer** (4 subunits: 2α, 2β). O₂ binding to one subunit causes a **conformational change** (Tense state → Relaxed state) that **increases the affinity** of the remaining subunits for O₂. This is called **Positive Cooperativity** or the **Hill Effect**. The result is the sigmoidal curve — flat at high $P_{O_2}$ (good loading) and steep at low $P_{O_2}$ (good unloading).

---

### 2.4 Factors Shifting the OHDC (KTU's Most Tested Concept)

A **rightward shift** = ↓ Hb affinity for O₂ = **easier unloading** at tissues.
A **leftward shift** = ↑ Hb affinity for O₂ = **harder unloading** but better loading.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic: "CADET, face Right!"** — *Channelopathy mnemonic; use what's tested.*
> **More accurate: "Exercise → Right shift"**
> Factors causing **rightward shift (↓ affinity):**
> - ↑ **C**O₂
> - ↑ **A**cidity (↓ pH)
> - ↑ **2,3-DPG** (2,3-Bisphosphoglycerate)
> - ↑ **E**xercise (↑ temperature)
> - ↑ **T**emperature
>
> The **Bohr Effect** is the combined effect of ↑ CO₂ and ↓ pH on O₂ unloading.

---

### 2.5 Carbon Dioxide Transport — Three Parallel Pathways

CO₂ is more soluble than O₂ (~24× more soluble in plasma) and is transported in **three forms**:

| Form | % of Total CO₂ Transport | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Dissolved CO₂** | **5–10%** | Henry's Law (physically dissolved) |
| **Bicarbonate Ion (HCO₃⁻)** | **60–70%** | Reversible hydration via Carbonic Anhydrase |
| **Carbamino Compounds** | **20–30%** | Bound to Hb (Carbaminohemoglobin) and plasma proteins |

---

### 2.6 The Bicarbonate Mechanism (The Dominant Pathway for CO₂)

In the **tissue capillaries** (high $P_{CO_2}$, ~46 mmHg):

**Step 1:** CO₂ diffuses from tissues into the RBC.

**Step 2:** Inside the RBC, the enzyme **Carbonic Anhydrase (CA)** catalyzes:

$$CO_2 + H_2O \xrightleftharpoons{CA} H_2CO_3 \rightleftharpoons H^+ + HCO_3^-$$

**Step 3:** The HCO₃⁻ ion is **actively pumped out** of the RBC into plasma via the **Band 3 Anion Exchange Protein (AE1)** in exchange for Cl⁻ — this is the **"Chloride Shift"** (Hamburger Shift).

**Step 4:** The H⁺ ions produced are **buffered by Hemoglobin** — Hb acts as a **buffer** because it has histidine residues that bind H⁺. The reaction:

$$HbO_2 + H^+ \rightleftharpoons HHb + O_2$$

This is the molecular basis of the **Haldane Effect**: deoxygenated Hb binds more CO₂ and H⁺ than oxygenated Hb.

In the **pulmonary capillaries** (low $P_{CO_2}$, ~40 mmHg), all reactions reverse: HCO₃⁻ re-enters the RBC, combines with H⁺ to reform H₂CO₃, which is dehydrated back to CO₂ and exhaled.

---

### 2.7 The Haldane Effect

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The Haldane Effect describes the phenomenon that **deoxygenated hemoglobin** (in peripheral tissues) has a **greater capacity to carry CO₂** (as carbaminohemoglobin and HCO₃⁻ buffer) than **oxygenated hemoglobin** (in lungs). This is the *physiological mirror* of the Bohr Effect.

**Mechanism in one line:** O₂ and CO₂ (and H⁺) compete for the same Hb binding sites, and their binding is **mutually exclusive** at the same allosteric locus.

---

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{dissolved} = \alpha \cdot P$ | mL gas / dL blood | Henry's Law for any gas |
| 2 | $C_{aO_2} = \alpha_{O_2} P_{aO_2} + [Hb] \cdot 1.34 \cdot S_{aO_2}$ | mL O₂/dL | Total arterial O₂ content |
| 3 | $C_{vO_2} = \alpha_{O_2} P_{vO_2} + [Hb] \cdot 1.34 \cdot S_{vO_2}$ | mL O₂/dL | Total venous O₂ content |
| 4 | $V_{O_2} = Q \times (C_{aO_2} - C_{vO_2})$ | mL O₂/min | Fick Principle for O₂ delivery |
| 5 | $CO_2 + H_2O \xrightleftharpoons{CA} H_2CO_3 \rightleftharpoons H^+ + HCO_3^-$ | mol/L | Carbonic anhydrase reaction |
| 6 | $V_{CO_2} = Q \times (C_{vCO_2} - C_{aCO_2})$ | mL CO₂/min | Fick Principle for CO₂ removal |
| 7 | Henderson-Hasselbalch: $pH = pK_a + \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[HCO_3^-]}{[CO_2]}\right)$ | dimensionless | Blood pH regulation |
| 8 | Fick's 1st Law: $J = -D \cdot \dfrac{\Delta C}{\Delta x}$ | mol/(m²·s) | Diffusion across alveolar membrane |
| 9 | $J_{gas} = \dfrac{A \cdot D \cdot (P_1 - P_2)}{T}$ | mL/min | Gas diffusion rate through tissue |
| 10 | $P_{50}$ reference value | **26.6 mmHg** | Standard OHDC reference |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Note:** $\alpha_{O_2} = 0.0031$ and $\alpha_{CO_2} = 0.067$ — note the **22× difference**. This is why CO₂ is far more readily carried in dissolved form than O₂.

---

### 2.9 Real-World Biomedical Engineering Applications

- **Pulse Oximeter (SpO₂ Sensor):** Measures $S_{aO_2}$ non-invasively by differential absorption of red (660 nm) and infrared (940 nm) light by Hb vs. HbO₂ — directly leveraging OHDC principles.
- **Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation (ECMO):** Artificial oxygenator design depends on **Fick's Law** across a synthetic membrane.
- **Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy (HBOT):** Increases dissolved O₂ (Henry's Law — pressure ↑ → dissolved gas ↑) for treatment of CO poisoning, decompression sickness.
- **Blood Gas Analyzers:** Measure PaO₂, PaCO₂, pH, [HCO₃⁻] using Severinghaus electrode and optical sensors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Oxygen Content in Arterial Blood

**Goal:** Derive the equation $C_{aO_2} = \alpha_{O_2} P_{aO_2} + [Hb] \cdot 1.34 \cdot S_{aO_2}$ from first principles.

**Step 1 — Begin with Henry's Law for dissolved O₂.**

By Henry's Law, the amount of O₂ physically dissolved in plasma is directly proportional to the partial pressure of O₂ in equilibrium with the plasma.

$$C_{O_2,\,dissolved} = \alpha_{O_2} \cdot P_{O_2}$$

**Step 2 — Express the Hb-bound O₂.**

Each gram of Hb can bind a maximum of 1.34 mL O₂ at full saturation. If the fractional saturation is $S_{aO_2}$, the actual bound O₂ per gram is:

$$C_{O_2,\,bound\,per\,g\,Hb} = 1.34 \cdot S_{aO_2} \text{ (mL O}_2\text{/g)}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by [Hb] concentration to get the total Hb-bound O₂ per dL of blood.**

$$C_{O_2,\,Hb} = [Hb] \cdot 1.34 \cdot S_{aO_2}$$

**Step 4 — Add the two parallel transport pathways.**

Since O₂ is transported simultaneously by both mechanisms (dissolved and Hb-bound), the total O₂ content is the sum:

$$C_{aO_2} = (\alpha_{O_2} \cdot P_{aO_2}) + ([Hb] \cdot 1.34 \cdot S_{aO_2})$$

**Step 5 — Substitute standard numerical values.**

With $\alpha_{O_2} = 0.0031$ mL/(dL·mmHg), $P_{aO_2} = 100$ mmHg, $[Hb] = 15$ g/dL, $S_{aO_2} = 0.97$:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{aO_2} &= (0.0031 \times 100) + (15 \times 1.34 \times 0.97) \\
&= 0.31 + 19.49 \\
&= 19.80 \text{ mL O}_2\text{/dL blood}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The dissolved O₂ fraction (0.31 mL/dL) is only **~1.6%** of the total. The Hb-bound fraction dominates — this is why **anemia** (low [Hb]) is clinically devastating even with normal lungs.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Fick's First Law Applied to Alveolar Gas Exchange

**Goal:** Derive the equation governing O₂ diffusion from alveolus to pulmonary capillary.

**Step 1 — State Fick's First Law of Diffusion.**

The flux $J$ of a substance across a membrane is proportional to the concentration gradient and inversely proportional to the membrane thickness.

$$J = -D \cdot \dfrac{\Delta C}{\Delta x}$$

Where:
- $J$ = diffusion flux (mL gas / min / cm²)
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient (cm²/s)
- $\Delta C$ = concentration difference across membrane
- $\Delta x$ = membrane thickness

**Step 2 — Convert concentration to partial pressure using Henry's Law.**

Since $C = \alpha \cdot P$, we can substitute to express flux in terms of **partial pressure difference** (which is what we can directly measure with a blood gas analyzer).

$$J_{gas} = \dfrac{A \cdot D \cdot \alpha \cdot (P_1 - P_2)}{T}$$

Where:
- $A$ = surface area of alveolar membrane
- $T$ = thickness of alveolar-capillary membrane
- $P_1 - P_2$ = partial pressure gradient

**Step 3 — Simplify to the standard form used in respiratory physiology.**

By grouping constants, we define the **Diffusing Capacity** $D_L$ of the lung for a given gas:

$$D_L = \dfrac{A \cdot D \cdot \alpha}{T}$$

Therefore:

$$V_{gas} = D_L \cdot (P_{alveolar} - P_{capillary})$$

**Step 4 — Apply to O₂ at rest.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{O_2} &= D_{L,O_2} \times (P_{AO_2} - P_{cap\,O_2}) \\
&= 21 \text{ mL/min/mmHg} \times (104 - 40) \\
&= 21 \times 64 \\
&= 1344 \text{ mL O}_2\text{/min} \text{ (max diffusing capacity)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physiological reserve:** At rest, the body only consumes ~250 mL O₂/min, but the lung can deliver up to 1344 mL/min — a **5× safety margin** during exercise. This explains why even patients with significant lung disease may have normal resting gas exchange.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: The Bicarbonate Buffer System (Henderson-Hasselbalch for CO₂)

**Step 1 — Start with the carbonic acid dissociation equilibrium.**

$$H_2CO_3 \rightleftharpoons H^+ + HCO_3^-$$

**Step 2 — Apply the acid dissociation constant.**

$$K_a = \dfrac{[H^+][HCO_3^-]}{[H_2CO_3]}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for [H⁺].**

$$[H^+] = \dfrac{K_a \cdot [H_2CO_3]}{[HCO_3^-]}$$

**Step 4 — Take the negative logarithm of both sides (definition of pH and pKₐ).**

$$pH = pK_a + \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[HCO_3^-]}{[H_2CO_3]}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $H_2CO_3 \approx 0.03 \times P_{CO_2}$ (Henry's Law for CO₂).**

$$pH = pK_a + \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[HCO_3^-]}{0.03 \times P_{CO_2}}\right)$$

With $pK_a = 6.1$, normal $[HCO_3^-] = 24$ mEq/L, normal $P_{CO_2} = 40$ mmHg:

$$\begin{aligned}
pH &= 6.1 + \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{24}{0.03 \times 40}\right) \\
&= 6.1 + \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{24}{1.2}\right) \\
&= 6.1 + \log_{10}(20) \\
&= 6.1 + 1.301 \\
&= 7.401
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters clinically:** Any change in $P_{CO_2}$ (ventilation problem) or $[HCO_3^-]$ (metabolic/renal problem) directly shifts blood pH. This is the basis of **respiratory acidosis/alkalosis** and **metabolic acidosis/alkalosis** diagnosis — a routine KTU long-answer topic.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Net CO₂ Transport via Bicarbonate (Mass Balance)

**Step 1 — At the tissues, CO₂ enters RBC and hydrates.**

$$\text{Rate} = k_1 \cdot [CO_2]_{tissue} - k_2 \cdot [H_2CO_3]$$

**Step 2 — Assume rapid equilibrium (catalyzed by Carbonic Anhydrase).**

At steady state, the forward and reverse rates are equal. From the equilibrium:

$$[H_2CO_3] = \dfrac{[H^+][HCO_3^-]}{K_a}$$

**Step 3 — Net CO₂ carried as HCO₃⁻ from tissue to lung (per dL of blood).**

$$\Delta C_{CO_2} = [HCO_3^-]_{venous} - [HCO_3^-]_{arterial} = 26 - 24 = 2 \text{ mEq/dL}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mL CO₂/dL using molar volume (22.4 mL/mmol at STP, but 1 mEq of HCO₃⁻ ≈ 22.4 mL CO₂ released).**

$$V_{CO_2\,from\,HCO_3} = 2 \text{ mEq} \times 22.4 = 44.8 \text{ mL CO}_2\text{/dL}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check:** The total CO₂ transported is ~48 mL/dL of blood, of which HCO₃⁻ accounts for ~45 mL/dL → **~93%** of CO₂ in transit is in the HCO₃⁻ form. This is consistent with the 60–70% textbook range because the textbook value is based on **% of total CO₂ produced by metabolism**, not the total HCO₃⁻ pool.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example: Shift in OHDC with Increased Temperature

**Problem:** A patient has a fever of 41°C (vs. normal 37°C). By approximately how much does the P₅₀ shift, and what is the physiological consequence?

**Step 1 — Recall the empirical relation:** OHDC shifts right by approximately **−2.5%** in P₅₀ per +1°C rise in temperature (or +0.5 mmHg per °C).

**Step 2 — Compute the new P₅₀.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= 41 - 37 = 4\,°C \\
\Delta P_{50} &= 26.6 \times 0.025 \times 4 = 2.66 \text{ mmHg (rightward shift)} \\
P_{50,\text{new}} &= 26.6 + 2.66 = 29.26 \text{ mmHg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**

A rightward shift means at any given $P_{O_2}$, Hb saturation is **lower** → more O₂ unloaded at the tissues. This is **physiologically adaptive** during fever because metabolic demand is increased.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's tip:** If a question asks "Why does exercise cause a rightward shift in OHDC?" — answer with **all four mechanisms**: (1) ↑ Temperature in active muscle, (2) ↑ CO₂ (Bohr), (3) ↓ pH (lactic acid + CO₂ → H⁺), (4) ↑ 2,3-DPG from chronic hypoxia.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: O₂ vs. CO₂ Transport Comparison

| Parameter | Oxygen (O₂) | Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) |
|---|---|---|
| Bunsen Solubility (α) | 0.0031 mL/dL/mmHg | 0.067 mL/dL/mmHg |
| Dissolved Fraction | ~1.5% | ~5–10% |
| Primary Carrier | Hemoglobin (Hb) | Bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻) |
| Binding Site on Hb | Fe²⁺ of heme group | N-terminal amino groups |
| Carbamino form | Oxyhemoglobin (HbO₂) | Carbaminohemoglobin (HbCO₂) |
| Direction of Diffusion | Alveoli → Blood → Tissues | Tissues → Blood → Alveoli |
| Effect of ↑ PCO₂ / ↓ pH | Right shift (Bohr) | ↑ Carriage (Haldane) |
| Gradient Magnitude | ~60 mmHg (104 → 40) | ~6 mmHg (46 → 40) |
| Pulmonary Diffusing Capacity (D_L) | 21 mL/min/mmHg | ~400 mL/min/mmHg |
| Forms carbamino compounds? | No | Yes |

---

### 3.7 Calculation: Cardiac Output from Fick Principle

**Problem:** A patient has $C_{aO_2} = 20$ mL/dL, $C_{vO_2} = 15$ mL/dL, and whole-body O₂ consumption $V_{O_2} = 250$ mL/min. Calculate cardiac output.

**Step 1 — State the Fick Principle for O₂.**

$$V_{O_2} = Q \times (C_{aO_2} - C_{vO_2})$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for Q.**

$$Q = \dfrac{V_{O_2}}{C_{aO_2} - C_{vO_2}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values (with unit conversion: mL/dL → mL/L by ×10).**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{aO_2} - C_{vO_2} &= (20 - 15) \text{ mL/dL} = 50 \text{ mL/L} \\
Q &= \dfrac{250 \text{ mL/min}}{50 \text{ mL/L}} = 5 \text{ L/min}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **classic Fick Principle** application, tested routinely in KTU boards. A normal resting cardiac output is **5 L/min**; during strenuous exercise, it can rise to 20–25 L/min — a **4–5× reserve**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Overview of Gas Transport Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ALV["ALVEOLUS"] -->|O2 diffuses P=104 mmHg| CAP["PULMONARY CAPILLARY"]
    TIS["TISSUE CELL MITOCHONDRIA"] -->|CO2 diffuses P=46 mmHg| VEN["VENOUS CAPILLARY"]
    CAP -->|O2 loaded onto Hb| RBC1["RBC WITH HbO2"]
    VEN -->|CO2 enters RBC| RBC2["RBC WITH HCO3"]
    RBC1 -->|Arterial blood O2=100 mmHg| ART["ARTERY"]
    RBC2 -->|Venous blood CO2=46 mmHg| VEIN["VEIN"]
    ART -->|Delivered to| TIS
    VEIN -->|Returns to| ALV
    RBC1 -.->|Bohr Effect| RBC2
    RBC2 -.->|Haldane Effect| RBC1
```

```mermaid
        DIFO2 -->|Enters RBC| RBO2["Red Blood Cell Interior"]
    end
    subgraph HBO2["HEMOGLOBIN BINDING"]
        RBO2 -->|Reversible Binding| HBO2B["Oxyhemoglobin HbO2 Formation"]
        HBO2B -->|Cooperativity| COOPO2["4 O2 per Hb Tetramer"]
        COOPO2 -->|Saturation SO2| SAGO2["Arterial Saturation 97 percent"]
    end
    subgraph UNLO2["OXYGEN UNLOADING AT TISSUES"]
        SAGO2 -->|Tissue PO2 20 mmHg| UNLBO2["O2 Released from Hb"]
        UNLBO2 -->|Bohr Effect Facilitation| DIFTO2["Diffuses into Mitochondria"]
    end
```

### 4.3 Carbon Dioxide Transport Block Diagram (The Three Pathways)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TISCO2["TISSUE CO2 GENERATION"]
        MET["Mitochondrial Respiration"] -->|CO2 produced| TCO2["Tissue PCO2 46 mmHg"]
    end
    subgraph RBTX["RBC INTERNAL PROCESSING"]
        TCO2 -->|Diffuses into RBC| RCO2["CO2 inside RBC"]
        RCO2 -->|5 to 10 percent Dissolved| DCO2["Physically Dissolved CO2"]
        RCO2 -->|Carbonic Anhydrase Catalysis| HYD["CO2 + H2O to H2CO3"]
        HYD -->|Dissociation| BIC["H+ and HCO3- ions"]
        RCO2 -->|20 to 30 percent Carbamino Binding| CARB["Carbaminohemoglobin HbCO2"]
    end
    subgraph PLTX["PLASMA TRANSPORT"]
        BIC -->|Chloride Shift Band 3 protein| PLBIC["HCO3- in Plasma 60 to 70 percent"]
        CARB -->|Bound to plasma proteins| PLCARB["Plasma Carbamino compounds"]
        DCO2 -->|Henry Law| PLDCO2["Dissolved CO2 in Plasma"]
    end
    subgraph LUCO2["LUNG EXCRETION"]
        PLBIC -->|Reenters RBC at lung| RCO2B["Reverse Chloride Shift"]
        RCO2B -->|Reconversion| CO2EXH["CO2 regenerated"]
        CO2EXH -->|Diffuses to alveolus| EXH["Exhaled Air PCO2 40 mmHg"]
    end
```

### 4.4 Oxygen-Hemoglobin Dissociation Curve Block Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEFT["LEFT SHIFT INCREASED AFFINITY"]
        LS1["Decreased PCO2"] --> LSO["Left Shift"]
        LS2["Increased pH Alkalosis"] --> LSO
        LS3["Decreased Temperature"] --> LSO
        LS4["Decreased 2,3-DPG"] --> LSO
        LS5["Fetal Hemoglobin HbF"] --> LSO
        LS6["Carbon Monoxide binding"] --> LSO
        LSO --> LSE["Effect Higher saturation at given PO2 Harder unloading"]
    end

        NRM --> NRT["PO2 26.6 mmHg SO2 50 percent P50 reference"]
    end

        RS1["Increased PCO2"] --> RSO["Right Shift"]
        RS2["Decreased pH Acidosis Bohr Effect"] --> RSO

        RS4["Increased 2,3-DPG Chronic Hypoxia Altitude"] --> RSO
        RS5["Increased BPG in stored blood"] --> RSO
        RSO --> RSE["Effect Lower saturation at given PO2 Easier unloading at tissues"]

    NORM --> LEFT
    NORM --> RIGHT
```

### 4.5 Bohr Effect vs. Haldane Effect — Conceptual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BO["BOHR EFFECT AT TISSUES"]
        BO1["Active tissues produce CO2"] --> BO2["PCO2 rises locally"]
        BO2 --> BO3["CO2 + H2O yields H2CO3 yields H+ + HCO3-"]
        BO3 --> BO4["Local pH drops"]
        BO4 --> BO5["Hb affinity for O2 decreases"]
        BO5 --> BO6["O2 released to mitochondria"]
    end

    end

```
